### [1] [Scaling Audio-Visual Quality Assessment Dataset via Crowdsourcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22659)
*Renyu Yang,Jian Jin,Lili Meng,Meiqin Liu,Yilin Wang,Balu Adsumilli,Weisi Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors propose a practical approach for constructing Audio-visual quality assessment (AVQA) datasets through crowdsourced subjective experiments, systematic data preparation, and extended annotations, creating YT-NTU-AVQ - the largest and most diverse AVQA dataset to date with 1,620 user-generated audio-video sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AVQA datasets are limited by small scale, insufficient diversity in content and quality, and lack of detailed annotations beyond overall scores, which hinders model development and multimodal perception research.

Method: The proposed approach includes: (1) a crowdsourced subjective experiment framework that breaks in-lab constraints, (2) a systematic data preparation strategy ensuring broad coverage of quality levels and semantic scenarios, and (3) extended annotations enabling research on multimodal perception mechanisms and their relation to content.

Result: The authors created YT-NTU-AVQ, the largest and most diverse AVQA dataset to date, containing 1,620 user-generated audio-video sequences, with the dataset and platform code available on GitHub.

Conclusion: This approach provides a practical solution for AVQA dataset construction that overcomes existing limitations and supports further research in multimodal perception and model development.

Abstract: Audio-visual quality assessment (AVQA) research has been stalled by limitations of existing datasets: they are typically small in scale, with insufficient diversity in content and quality, and annotated only with overall scores. These shortcomings provide limited support for model development and multimodal perception research. We propose a practical approach for AVQA dataset construction. First, we design a crowdsourced subjective experiment framework for AVQA, breaks the constraints of in-lab settings and achieves reliable annotation across varied environments. Second, a systematic data preparation strategy is further employed to ensure broad coverage of both quality levels and semantic scenarios. Third, we extend the dataset with additional annotations, enabling research on multimodal perception mechanisms and their relation to content. Finally, we validate this approach through YT-NTU-AVQ, the largest and most diverse AVQA dataset to date, consisting of 1,620 user-generated audio and video (A/V) sequences. The dataset and platform code are available at https://github.com/renyu12/YT-NTU-AVQ

</details>


### [2] [Enabling clinical use of foundation models in histopathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22347)
*Audun L. Henriksen,Ole-Johan Skrede,Lisa van der Schee,Enric Domingo,Sepp De Raedt,Ilyá Kostolomov,Jennifer Hay,Karolina Cyll,Wanja Kildal,Joakim Kalsnes,Robert W. Williams,Manohar Pradhan,John Arne Nesheim,Hanne A. Askautrud,Maria X. Isaksen,Karmele Saez de Gordoa,Miriam Cuatrecasas,Joanne Edwards,TransSCOT group,Arild Nesbakken,Neil A. Shepherd,Ian Tomlinson,Daniel-Christoph Wagner,Rachel S. Kerr,Tarjei Sveinsgjerd Hveem,Knut Liestøl,Yoshiaki Nakamura,Marco Novelli,Masaaki Miyo,Sebastian Foersch,David N. Church,Miangela M. Lacle,David J. Kerr,Andreas Kleppe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Foundation models in computational pathology often capture technical variability (scanner-specific, pre-analytic variations) that biases downstream models. This work introduces novel robustness losses during downstream task-specific model training to improve robustness while maintaining accuracy by focusing on biologically relevant features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current foundation models in histopathology capture both biologically relevant features and pre-analytic/scanner-specific variations, which bias predictions of downstream task-specific models. There is a need to mitigate this robustness issue without retraining foundation models themselves.

Method: A purpose-designed comprehensive experimentation setup with 27,042 Whole Slide Images (WSIs) from 6,155 patients. The study trains thousands of models using features extracted from eight popular foundation models for computational pathology. Novel robustness losses are introduced during training of downstream task-specific models to reduce sensitivity to technical variability.

Result: Substantial improvement in robustness to technical variability, with prediction accuracy improvements by focusing on biologically relevant features rather than technical artifacts. The approach successfully mitigates robustness issues of foundation models without retraining the foundation models themselves.

Conclusion: The proposed robustness losses enable the development of robust computational pathology models applicable to real-world data in routine clinical practice, bridging the gap between foundation models and practical clinical deployment.

Abstract: Foundation models in histopathology are expected to facilitate the development of high-performing and generalisable deep learning systems. However, current models capture not only biologically relevant features, but also pre-analytic and scanner-specific variation that bias the predictions of task-specific models trained from the foundation model features. Here we show that introducing novel robustness losses during training of downstream task-specific models reduces sensitivity to technical variability. A purpose-designed comprehensive experimentation setup with 27,042 WSIs from 6155 patients is used to train thousands of models from the features of eight popular foundation models for computational pathology. In addition to a substantial improvement in robustness, we observe that prediction accuracy improves by focusing on biologically relevant features. Our approach successfully mitigates robustness issues of foundation models for computational pathology without retraining the foundation models themselves, enabling development of robust computational pathology models applicable to real-world data in routine clinical practice.

</details>


### [3] [Optimizing Neural Network Architecture for Medical Image Segmentation Using Monte Carlo Tree Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22361)
*Liping Meng,Fan Nie,Yunyun Zhang,Chao Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MNAS-Unet combines Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and Neural Architecture Search (NAS) for efficient medical image segmentation, achieving 54% search budget reduction and superior accuracy on PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance the efficiency and accuracy of architecture search for medical image segmentation, optimize DownSC and UpSC unit structures for fast and precise model adjustments.

Method: Proposes MNAS-Unet framework combining MCTS for dynamic architecture exploration and NAS, with optimized DownSC and UpSC unit structures.

Result: Outperforms NAS-Unet and SOTA models on PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS datasets; achieves 54% search budget reduction (139 vs 300 epochs), 0.6M parameters, and lower GPU memory consumption.

Conclusion: MNAS-Unet improves search efficiency while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints, enhancing practical applicability.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel medical image segmentation framework, MNAS-Unet, which combines Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) and Neural Architecture Search (NAS). MNAS-Unet dynamically explores promising network architectures through MCTS, significantly enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of architecture search. It also optimizes the DownSC and UpSC unit structures, enabling fast and precise model adjustments. Experimental results demonstrate that MNAS-Unet outperforms NAS-Unet and other state-of-the-art models in segmentation accuracy on several medical image datasets, including PROMISE12, Ultrasound Nerve, and CHAOS. Furthermore, compared with NAS-Unet, MNAS-Unet reduces the architecture search budget by 54% (early stopping at 139 epochs versus 300 epochs under the same search setting), while achieving a lightweight model with only 0.6M parameters and lower GPU memory consumption, which further improves its practical applicability. These results suggest that MNAS-Unet can improve search efficiency while maintaining competitive segmentation accuracy under practical resource constraints.

</details>


### [4] [AeroDGS: Physically Consistent Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Single-Sequence Aerial 4D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22376)
*Hanyang Liu,Rongjun Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AeroDGS is a physics-guided 4D Gaussian splatting framework for monocular UAV videos that uses a Monocular Geometry Lifting module and Physics-Guided Optimization with ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors to address depth ambiguity and unstable motion estimation in aerial reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 4D scene reconstruction approaches are limited under aerial conditions with single-view capture, wide spatial range, and dynamic objects of limited spatial footprint with large motion disparity, which cause severe depth ambiguity and unstable motion estimation, making monocular aerial reconstruction inherently ill-posed.

Method: AeroDGS introduces a Monocular Geometry Lifting module to reconstruct reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence, and a Physics-Guided Optimization module incorporating differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors to resolve monocular ambiguity and transform image cues into physically consistent motion. The framework jointly refines static backgrounds and dynamic entities with stable geometry and coherent temporal evolution.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real UAV scenes demonstrate that AeroDGS outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity in dynamic aerial environments.

Conclusion: AeroDGS effectively addresses the challenges of monocular aerial reconstruction by combining geometric lifting with physics-guided priors, enabling high-fidelity 4D reconstruction from single-view UAV videos across various altitudes and motion conditions.

Abstract: Recent advances in 4D scene reconstruction have significantly improved dynamic modeling across various domains. However, existing approaches remain limited under aerial conditions with single-view capture, wide spatial range, and dynamic objects of limited spatial footprint and large motion disparity. These challenges cause severe depth ambiguity and unstable motion estimation, making monocular aerial reconstruction inherently ill-posed. To this end, we present AeroDGS, a physics-guided 4D Gaussian splatting framework for monocular UAV videos. AeroDGS introduces a Monocular Geometry Lifting module that reconstructs reliable static and dynamic geometry from a single aerial sequence, providing a robust basis for dynamic estimation. To further resolve monocular ambiguity, we propose a Physics-Guided Optimization module that incorporates differentiable ground-support, upright-stability, and trajectory-smoothness priors, transforming ambiguous image cues into physically consistent motion. The framework jointly refines static backgrounds and dynamic entities with stable geometry and coherent temporal evolution. We additionally build a real-world UAV dataset that spans various altitudes and motion conditions to evaluate dynamic aerial reconstruction. Experiments on synthetic and real UAV scenes demonstrate that AeroDGS outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior reconstruction fidelity in dynamic aerial environments.

</details>


### [5] [Enhancing Renal Tumor Malignancy Prediction: Deep Learning with Automatic 3D CT Organ Focused Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22381)
*Zhengkang Fan,Chengkun Sun,Russell Terry,Jie Xu,Longin Jan Latecki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deep learning framework for renal tumor malignancy prediction using 3D CT images with an Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss function that eliminates the need for manual tumor segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate malignancy prediction is crucial for clinical decision-making, but existing imaging modalities lack sufficient accuracy. Traditional deep learning approaches rely on manual tumor segmentation, which is labor-intensive, costly, and requires expert knowledge. The authors aim to improve predictive accuracy without requiring explicit segmentation input.

Method: The study developed a deep learning framework with an Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss function that modifies the attention mechanism of image patches so that organ patches attend only to other organ patches. This design enables malignancy prediction without requiring manual segmentation of the tumor region in 3D renal CT images at deployment time.

Result: The framework achieved an AUC of 0.685 and F1-score of 0.872 on a private UF IDR dataset, and AUC of 0.760 and F1-score of 0.852 on the publicly available KiTS21 dataset. These results surpass conventional segmentation-based cropping models.

Conclusion: The OFA-based approach offers a more efficient and reliable method for malignancy prediction without explicit segmentation, thereby enhancing clinical decision-making in renal cancer diagnosis.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of malignancy in renal tumors is crucial for informing clinical decisions and optimizing treatment strategies. However, existing imaging modalities lack the necessary accuracy to reliably predict malignancy before surgical intervention. While deep learning has shown promise in malignancy prediction using 3D CT images, traditional approaches often rely on manual segmentation to isolate the tumor region and reduce noise, which enhances predictive performance. Manual segmentation, however, is labor-intensive, costly, and dependent on expert knowledge. In this study, a deep learning framework was developed utilizing an Organ Focused Attention (OFA) loss function to modify the attention of image patches so that organ patches attend only to other organ patches. Hence, no segmentation of 3D renal CT images is required at deployment time for malignancy prediction. The proposed framework achieved an AUC of 0.685 and an F1-score of 0.872 on a private dataset from the UF Integrated Data Repository (IDR), and an AUC of 0.760 and an F1-score of 0.852 on the publicly available KiTS21 dataset. These results surpass the performance of conventional models that rely on segmentation-based cropping for noise reduction, demonstrating the frameworks ability to enhance predictive accuracy without explicit segmentation input. The findings suggest that this approach offers a more efficient and reliable method for malignancy prediction, thereby enhancing clinical decision-making in renal cancer diagnosis.

</details>


### [6] [SwiftNDC: Fast Neural Depth Correction for High-Fidelity 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22565)
*Kang Han,Wei Xiang,Lu Yu,Mathew Wyatt,Gaowen Liu,Ramana Rao Kompella*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SwiftNDC is a fast depth-guided 3D reconstruction framework that uses a Neural Depth Correction field to generate cross-view consistent depth maps, producing a dense point cloud initialization that accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting and improves both mesh reconstruction and novel-view synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing depth-guided 3D reconstruction methods suffer from scale drift, multi-view inconsistencies, and require substantial refinement to achieve high-fidelity geometry.

Method: SwiftNDC builds around a Neural Depth Correction field that produces cross-view consistent depth maps. From these refined depths, it generates a dense point cloud through back-projection and robust reprojection-error filtering, providing a clean and uniformly distributed geometric initialization for downstream reconstruction.

Result: SwiftNDC consistently reduces running time for accurate mesh reconstruction and boosts rendering fidelity for view synthesis across five datasets (two for mesh reconstruction, three for novel-view synthesis). It enables high-quality surfaces with significantly fewer optimization iterations for mesh reconstruction and improves 3DGS rendering quality for novel-view synthesis.

Conclusion: SwiftNDC demonstrates the effectiveness of combining neural depth refinement with robust geometric initialization for high-fidelity and efficient 3D reconstruction. The method provides both speed and quality improvements, making it a valuable contribution to the field.

Abstract: Depth-guided 3D reconstruction has gained popularity as a fast alternative to optimization-heavy approaches, yet existing methods still suffer from scale drift, multi-view inconsistencies, and the need for substantial refinement to achieve high-fidelity geometry. Here, we propose SwiftNDC, a fast and general framework built around a Neural Depth Correction field that produces cross-view consistent depth maps. From these refined depths, we generate a dense point cloud through back-projection and robust reprojection-error filtering, obtaining a clean and uniformly distributed geometric initialization for downstream reconstruction. This reliable dense geometry substantially accelerates 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for mesh reconstruction, enabling high-quality surfaces with significantly fewer optimization iterations. For novel-view synthesis, SwiftNDC can also improve 3DGS rendering quality, highlighting the benefits of strong geometric initialization. We conduct a comprehensive study across five datasets, including two for mesh reconstruction, as well as three for novel-view synthesis. SwiftNDC consistently reduces running time for accurate mesh reconstruction and boosts rendering fidelity for view synthesis, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining neural depth refinement with robust geometric initialization for high-fidelity and efficient 3D reconstruction.

</details>


### [7] [Vision Transformers Need More Than Registers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22394)
*Cheng Shi,Yizhou Yu,Sibei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies that Vision Transformer artifacts originate from lazy aggregation behavior where semantically irrelevant background patches are used as shortcuts for global semantics, driven by global attention and coarse-grained supervision. The authors propose a solution that selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token to reduce background-dominated shortcuts, consistently improving performance across 12 benchmarks under different supervision paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Vision Transformers (ViTs) pre-trained on large-scale data provide general-purpose representations for diverse downstream tasks, artifacts are widely observed across different supervision paradigms and downstream tasks. The fundamental mechanisms behind these artifacts have yet to be sufficiently elucidated.

Method: Through systematic analysis, the authors identify that ViT artifacts originate from lazy aggregation behavior: ViT uses semantically irrelevant background patches as shortcuts to represent global semantics, driven by global attention and coarse-grained semantic supervision. Their solution selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token to reduce the influence of background-dominated shortcuts.

Result: The proposed solution consistently improves performance across 12 benchmarks under label-, text-, and self-supervision paradigms, demonstrating effectiveness across different supervision settings.

Conclusion: This work provides a new perspective on understanding ViT behavior and offers insights into mitigating artifacts in Vision Transformers through selective patch feature integration.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs), when pre-trained on large-scale data, provide general-purpose representations for diverse downstream tasks. However, artifacts in ViTs are widely observed across different supervision paradigms and downstream tasks. Through systematic analysis of artifacts in ViTs, we find that their fundamental mechanisms have yet to be sufficiently elucidated. In this paper, through systematic analysis, we conclude that these artifacts originate from a lazy aggregation behavior: ViT uses semantically irrelevant background patches as shortcuts to represent global semantics, driven by global attention and Coarse-grained semantic supervision. Our solution selectively integrates patch features into the CLS token, reducing the influence of background-dominated shortcuts and consistently improving performance across 12 benchmarks under label-, text-, and self-supervision. We hope this work offers a new perspective on ViT behavior.

</details>


### [8] [CLIP Is Shortsighted: Paying Attention Beyond the First Sentence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22419)
*Marc-Antoine Lavoie,Anas Mahmoud,Aldo Zaimi,Arsene Fansi Tchango,Steven L. Waslander*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeBias-CLIP addresses the bias in CLIP models caused by the summary sentence at the beginning of long captions. By removing this summary sentence and distributing supervision across all token positions, DeBias-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improves short-text retrieval, and is less sensitive to sentence order permutations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP models learn transferable multi-modal features via image-text contrastive learning, but their pretraining is dominated by short captions that bias the model toward encoding simple descriptions. Recent work mitigates this by fine-tuning on small-scale long-caption datasets, but both human- and LLM-generated long captions typically begin with a one-sentence summary followed by a detailed description. This summary sentence acts as a shortcut during training, concentrating attention on the opening sentence and early tokens while weakening alignment over the rest of the caption.

Method: DeBias-CLIP removes the summary sentence during training and applies sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions. It is a drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP with no additional trainable parameters.

Result: DeBias-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improves short-text retrieval, and is less sensitive to sentence order permutations.

Conclusion: By addressing the common bias in caption structure where summaries appear at the beginning of long captions, DeBias-CLIP effectively distributes supervision across all token positions, leading to improved performance on both short-text and long-text retrieval tasks.

Abstract: CLIP models learn transferable multi-modal features via image-text contrastive learning on internet-scale data. They are widely used in zero-shot classification, multi-modal retrieval, text-to-image diffusion, and as image encoders in large vision-language models. However, CLIP's pretraining is dominated by images paired with short captions, biasing the model toward encoding simple descriptions of salient objects and leading to coarse alignment on complex scenes and dense descriptions. While recent work mitigates this by fine-tuning on small-scale long-caption datasets, we identify an important common bias: both human- and LLM-generated long captions typically begin with a one-sentence summary followed by a detailed description. We show that this acts as a shortcut during training, concentrating attention on the opening sentence and early tokens and weakening alignment over the rest of the caption. To resolve this, we introduce DeBias-CLIP, which removes the summary sentence during training and applies sentence sub-sampling and text token padding to distribute supervision across all token positions. DeBias-CLIP achieves state-of-the-art long-text retrieval, improves short-text retrieval, and is less sensitive to sentence order permutations. It is a drop-in replacement for Long-CLIP with no additional trainable parameters.

</details>


### [9] [SimpleOCR: Rendering Visualized Questions to Teach MLLMs to Read](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22426)
*Yibo Peng,Peng Xia,Ding Zhong,Kaide Zeng,Siwei Han,Yiyang Zhou,Jiaqi Liu,Ruiyi Zhang,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Visualized-Question (VQ) diagnostic setting to test whether MLLMs genuinely read text in images or rely on parametric shortcuts. They find models exhibit "modality laziness" (12.7% performance drop in VQ) and propose SimpleOCR, a plug-and-play training strategy that forces visual text extraction by transforming samples into VQ format with randomized styles, achieving 5.4% gains on OOD benchmarks with 30x less data than RL-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite rapid MLLM advancements, a critical unanswered question remains: do these models genuinely "read" text embedded in images, or do they merely rely on parametric shortcuts in the text prompt? The authors seek to diagnose this issue to understand if models are truly processing visual text or taking lazy shortcuts.

Method: The authors introduce the Visualized-Question (VQ) setting where text queries are rendered directly onto images to structurally mandate visual engagement. They diagnose Qwen2.5-VL models, finding they suffer a 12.7% performance degradation in VQ mode, exposing "modality laziness." To address this, they propose SimpleOCR: a plug-and-play training strategy that transforms training samples into VQ format with randomized styles, imposing structural constraints to invalidate text-based shortcuts and force activation of visual text extraction pathways.

Result: SimpleOCR yields robust gains without architectural modifications. On four OOD benchmarks, it surpasses the base model by 5.4% and GRPO-based methods by 2.7%. It exhibits extreme data efficiency, achieving superior performance with 30x fewer samples (8.5K) compared to recent RL-based methods, and seamlessly integrates with advanced RL strategies like NoisyRollout.

Conclusion: This work exposes the "modality laziness" in MLLMs where models fail to genuinely engage with visual text despite strong OCR capabilities. SimpleOCR provides an effective solution by imposing structural constraints that force models to activate visual text extraction pathways, demonstrating that significant performance improvements are possible through targeted training strategies rather than architectural changes, with substantial data efficiency advantages.

Abstract: Despite the rapid advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), a critical question regarding their visual grounding mechanism remains unanswered: do these models genuinely ``read'' text embedded in images, or do they merely rely on parametric shortcuts in the text prompt? In this work, we diagnose this issue by introducing the Visualized-Question (VQ) setting, where text queries are rendered directly onto images to structurally mandate visual engagement. Our diagnostic experiments on Qwen2.5-VL reveal a startling capability-utilization gap: despite possessing strong OCR capabilities, models suffer a performance degradation of up to 12.7% in the VQ setting, exposing a deep-seated ``modality laziness.'' To bridge this gap, we propose SimpleOCR, a plug-and-play training strategy that imposes a structural constraint on the learning process. By transforming training samples into the VQ format with randomized styles, SimpleOCR effectively invalidates text-based shortcuts, compelling the model to activate and optimize its visual text extraction pathways. Empirically, SimpleOCR yields robust gains without architectural modifications. On four representative OOD benchmarks, it surpasses the base model by 5.4% and GRPO based on original images by 2.7%, while exhibiting extreme data efficiency, achieving superior performance with 30x fewer samples (8.5K) than recent RL-based methods. Furthermore, its plug-and-play nature allows seamless integration with advanced RL strategies like NoisyRollout to yield complementary improvements. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleOCR.

</details>


### [10] [Exploring Multimodal LMMs for Online Episodic Memory Question Answering on the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22455)
*Giuseppe Lando,Rosario Forte,Antonino Furnari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper investigates using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for real-time online episodic memory question answering on edge devices, achieving competitive performance (51.76-54.40% accuracy) compared to cloud-based solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud offloading raises privacy and latency concerns for wearable assistants; the authors investigate edge implementation to enable privacy-preserving episodic memory retrieval.

Method: A two-thread asynchronous pipeline with a Descriptor Thread that converts video to lightweight textual memory and a Question Answering Thread that reasons over the memory to answer queries, tested on the QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark under streaming constraints.

Result: Consumer-grade 8GB GPU: 51.76% accuracy with 0.41s TTFT; enterprise-grade server: 54.40% accuracy with 0.88s TTFT; cloud-based solution: 56.00% accuracy.

Conclusion: Edge-based solutions show promising potential for privacy-preserving episodic memory retrieval, achieving competitive performance to cloud solutions.

Abstract: We investigate the feasibility of using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for real-time online episodic memory question answering. While cloud offloading is common, it raises privacy and latency concerns for wearable assistants, hence we investigate implementation on the edge. We integrated streaming constraints into our question answering pipeline, which is structured into two asynchronous threads: a Descriptor Thread that continuously converts video into a lightweight textual memory, and a Question Answering (QA) Thread that reasons over the textual memory to answer queries. Experiments on the QAEgo4D-Closed benchmark analyze the performance of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) within strict resource boundaries, showing promising results also when compared to clound-based solutions. Specifically, an end-to-end configuration running on a consumer-grade 8GB GPU achieves 51.76% accuracy with a Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) of 0.41s. Scaling to a local enterprise-grade server yields 54.40% accuracy with a TTFT of 0.88s. In comparison, a cloud-based solution obtains an accuracy of 56.00%. These competitive results highlight the potential of edge-based solutions for privacy-preserving episodic memory retrieval.

</details>


### [11] [MammoWise: Multi-Model Local RAG Pipeline for Mammography Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22462)
*Raiyan Jahangir,Nafiz Imtiaz Khan,Amritanand Sudheerkumar,Vladimir Filkov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MammoWise is a local multi-model pipeline that transforms open-source Vision Language Models (VLMs) into mammogram report generators and multi-task classifiers, enabling privacy-preserving mammography documentation with zero-shot/few-shot prompting, RAG, and QLoRA fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mammography is high volume, time-sensitive, and documentation-heavy. Radiologists must translate subtle visual findings into consistent BI-RADS assessments, breast density categories, and structured narratives. Existing VLMs are limited by closed cloud systems, tightly coupled architectures, privacy concerns, and lack of reproducibility and adaptability.

Method: MammoWise is a local multi-model pipeline supporting any Ollama-hosted VLM and mammography dataset. It enables zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought prompting with optional multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) using a vector database. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of MedGemma is implemented for improved classification performance.

Result: Report generation is consistently strong and improves with few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but sensitive to model and dataset choice. QLoRA fine-tuning of MedGemma achieved BI-RADS accuracy of 0.7545, density accuracy of 0.8840, and calcification accuracy of 0.9341 while preserving report quality.

Conclusion: MammoWise provides a practical and extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting within a unified and reproducible workflow, addressing privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability limitations of existing solutions.

Abstract: Screening mammography is high volume, time sensitive, and documentation heavy. Radiologists must translate subtle visual findings into consistent BI-RADS assessments, breast density categories, and structured narrative reports. While recent Vision Language Models (VLMs) enable image-to-text reporting, many rely on closed cloud systems or tightly coupled architectures that limit privacy, reproducibility, and adaptability. We present MammoWise, a local multi-model pipeline that transforms open source VLMs into mammogram report generators and multi-task classifiers. MammoWise supports any Ollama-hosted VLM and mammography dataset, and enables zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought prompting, with optional multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) using a vector database for case-specific context. We evaluate MedGemma, LLaVA-Med, and Qwen2.5-VL on VinDr-Mammo and DMID datasets, assessing report quality (BERTScore, ROUGE-L), BI-RADS classification, breast density, and key findings. Report generation is consistently strong and improves with few-shot prompting and RAG. Classification is feasible but sensitive to model and dataset choice. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) of MedGemma improves reliability, achieving BI-RADS accuracy of 0.7545, density accuracy of 0.8840, and calcification accuracy of 0.9341 while preserving report quality. MammoWise provides a practical and extensible framework for deploying local VLMs for mammography reporting within a unified and reproducible workflow.

</details>


### [12] [Beyond Dominant Patches: Spatial Credit Redistribution For Grounded Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22469)
*Niamul Hassan Samin,Md Arifur Rahman,Abdullah Ibne Hanif,Juena Ahmed Noshin,Md Ashikur Rahman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free inference-time intervention called Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR) reduces hallucinations in vision-language models by redistributing activation credit from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs, with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) frequently hallucinate objects absent from input images. The authors identify "spatial credit collapse" as the root cause: activation credit concentrates on sparse visual patches in early transformer layers, which suppresses contextual evidence and increases reliance on language priors.

Method: Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR): A training-free inference-time intervention that redistributes hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs. Attention-guided source selection is essential for effectiveness.

Result: SCR reduces hallucination by 4.7-6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, cuts CHAIR-s by 3.7-5.2 percentage points (42-51% relative) and CHAIR-i by 2.7-4.4 percentage points (44-58% relative), and preserves CIDEr within 0.8 percentage points. Overhead is only 43-56 ms, making it practical for real-time settings.

Conclusion: Attention-guided source selection is essential for SCR's effectiveness. Replacing it with uniform random selection reduces gains from ~4.7-6.0 to only ~2.6-3.4 percentage points. SCR Pareto-dominates OPERA, VCD, and OVCD on both hallucination rate and CIDEr, demonstrating that spatial credit collapse is a key driver of VLM hallucinations.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) frequently hallucinate objects absent from the input image. We trace this failure to spatial credit collapse: activation credit concentrating on sparse visual patches in early transformer layers, which suppresses contextual evidence and increases reliance on language priors. We introduce Spatial Credit Redistribution (SCR), a training-free inference-time intervention that redistributes hidden-state activation from high-attention source patches to their context, guided by low-entropy inputs. We evaluate six model families (Chameleon, LLaVA, and Qwen, including both Qwen-VL and Qwen2-VL) at scales of 7B, 13B, and 30B, on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks. SCR reduces hallucination by ~4.7-6.0 percentage points on POPE-Adversarial, cuts CHAIR-s by 3.7-5.2 percentage points (42-51 percent relative), and CHAIR-i by 2.7-4.4 percentage points (44-58 percent relative), and preserves CIDEr within 0.8 percentage points. Gains are largest for low-entropy inputs, consistent with the theoretical framework. SCR incurs only 43-56 ms overhead (small models: +43-46 ms; large models: +54-56 ms), roughly 3-6 times lower than OPERA and VCD and 1.3-1.7 times lower than OVCD (+72 ms), while Pareto-dominating all three on both hallucination rate and CIDEr, making it practical for real-time settings. A controlled ablation confirms that attention-guided source selection is essential: replacing it with uniform random selection reduces hallucination rate gains from ~4.7-6.0 percentage points to only ~2.6-3.4 percentage points, pointing to credit-collapse as the key driver.

</details>


### [13] [Pix2Key: Controllable Open-Vocabulary Retrieval with Semantic Decomposition and Self-Supervised Visual Dictionary Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22510)
*Guoyizhe Wei,Yang Jiao,Nan Xi,Zhishen Huang,Jingjing Meng,Rama Chellappa,Yan Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pix2Key introduces a novel approach for Composed Image Retrieval that uses open-vocabulary visual dictionaries for both queries and candidates, enabling intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking. A self-supervised pretraining component V-Dict-AE further enhances fine-grained attribute understanding without CIR-specific supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classic CIR methods suffer from losing fine-grained cues due to supervised triplets, while zero-shot approaches that caption reference images and merge with edits may miss implicit user intent and produce repetitive results.

Method: Pix2Key represents queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries in a unified embedding space, enabling intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking. A self-supervised pretraining component called V-Dict-AE improves dictionary representation using only images, strengthening fine-grained attribute understanding.

Result: On the DFMM-Compose benchmark, Pix2Key improves Recall@10 by up to 3.2 points. Adding V-Dict-AE provides an additional 2.3-point gain while improving intent consistency and maintaining high list diversity.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the limitations of existing CIR approaches by using visual dictionaries that capture both query intent and candidate diversity, achieving significant performance improvements over baseline methods.

Abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) uses a reference image plus a natural-language edit to retrieve images that apply the requested change while preserving other relevant visual content. Classic fusion pipelines typically rely on supervised triplets and can lose fine-grained cues, while recent zero-shot approaches often caption the reference image and merge the caption with the edit, which may miss implicit user intent and return repetitive results. We present Pix2Key, which represents both queries and candidates as open-vocabulary visual dictionaries, enabling intent-aware constraint matching and diversity-aware reranking in a unified embedding space. A self-supervised pretraining component, V-Dict-AE, further improves the dictionary representation using only images, strengthening fine-grained attribute understanding without CIR-specific supervision. On the DFMM-Compose benchmark, Pix2Key improves Recall@10 up to 3.2 points, and adding V-Dict-AE yields an additional 2.3-point gain while improving intent consistency and maintaining high list diversity.

</details>


### [14] [DisQ-HNet: A Disentangled Quantized Half-UNet for Interpretable Multimodal Image Synthesis Applications to Tau-PET Synthesis from T1 and FLAIR MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22545)
*Agamdeep S. Chopra,Caitlin Neher,Tianyi Ren,Juampablo E. Heras Rivera,Mehmet Kurt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DisQ-HNet is a framework that synthesizes tau-PET images from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI using Partial Information Decomposition (PID)-guided VQ-VAE encoding and Half-UNet decoding, achieving higher fidelity reconstruction and better preservation of disease-relevant signals for Alzheimer's disease tasks compared to baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tau-PET is expensive and limited in availability, so the authors seek MRI-based alternatives to provide an in vivo marker of Alzheimer's disease pathology.

Method: The method combines (i) a Partial Information Decomposition (PID)-guided vector-quantized encoder that partitions latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components, and (ii) a Half-UNet decoder that preserves anatomical detail using pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues rather than direct encoder feature reuse.

Result: Across multiple baselines (VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet), DisQ-HNet maintains reconstruction fidelity and better preserves disease-relevant signal for downstream AD tasks, including Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. PID-based Shapley analysis provides modality-specific attribution of synthesized uptake patterns.

Conclusion: DisQ-HNet successfully synthesizes tau-PET from MRI while offering interpretability through modality attribution, demonstrating that the framework can maintain reconstruction quality while better capturing disease-relevant features for Alzheimer's disease analysis.

Abstract: Tau positron emission tomography (tau-PET) provides an in vivo marker of Alzheimer's disease pathology, but cost and limited availability motivate MRI-based alternatives. We introduce DisQ-HNet (DQH), a framework that synthesizes tau-PET from paired T1-weighted and FLAIR MRI while exposing how each modality contributes to the prediction. The method combines (i) a Partial Information Decomposition (PID)-guided, vector-quantized encoder that partitions latent information into redundant, unique, and complementary components, and (ii) a Half-UNet decoder that preserves anatomical detail using pseudo-skip connections conditioned on structural edge cues rather than direct encoder feature reuse. Across multiple baselines (VAE, VQ-VAE, and UNet), DisQ-HNet maintains reconstruction fidelity and better preserves disease-relevant signal for downstream AD tasks, including Braak staging, tau localization, and classification. PID-based Shapley analysis provides modality-specific attribution of synthesized uptake patterns.

</details>


### [15] [DrivePTS: A Progressive Learning Framework with Textual and Structural Enhancement for Driving Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22549)
*Zhechao Wang,Yiming Zeng,Lufan Ma,Zeqing Fu,Chen Bai,Ziyao Lin,Cheng Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DrivePTS is a novel framework for synthesizing diverse driving scenes using diffusion models, addressing inter-condition dependency and insufficient semantic/structural details through progressive learning, Vision-Language Model guidance, and a frequency-guided structure loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scene synthesis methods aggregate HD maps and 3D bounding boxes in diffusion models, but suffer from: 1) implicit inter-condition dependency causing failures when conditions change independently, and 2) insufficient details in semantic (brief captions, weak background modeling) and structural (standard denoising loss neglects foreground structural details, causing blurriness) aspects.

Method: DrivePTS introduces three innovations: 1) Progressive learning strategy with explicit mutual information constraint to mitigate inter-condition dependency between geometric conditions; 2) Vision-Language Model to generate multi-view hierarchical descriptions across six semantic aspects for fine-grained textual guidance; 3) Frequency-guided structure loss to enhance sensitivity to high-frequency elements, improving foreground structural fidelity.

Result: DrivePTS achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scenes. It successfully generates rare scenes where prior methods fail, demonstrating strong generalization ability and superior performance over existing approaches.

Conclusion: DrivePTS effectively addresses the challenges of scene synthesis for autonomous driving by decoupling geometric conditions, providing rich semantic guidance, and preserving structural details. The proposed framework significantly outperforms current methods in both fidelity and controllability, with particular strength in generating rare and challenging scenarios that demonstrate robust generalization.

Abstract: Synthesis of diverse driving scenes serves as a crucial data augmentation technique for validating the robustness and generalizability of autonomous driving systems. Current methods aggregate high-definition (HD) maps and 3D bounding boxes as geometric conditions in diffusion models for conditional scene generation. However, implicit inter-condition dependency causes generation failures when control conditions change independently. Additionally, these methods suffer from insufficient details in both semantic and structural aspects. Specifically, brief and view-invariant captions restrict semantic contexts, resulting in weak background modeling. Meanwhile, the standard denoising loss with uniform spatial weighting neglects foreground structural details, causing visual distortions and blurriness. To address these challenges, we propose DrivePTS, which incorporates three key innovations. Firstly, our framework adopts a progressive learning strategy to mitigate inter-dependency between geometric conditions, reinforced by an explicit mutual information constraint. Secondly, a Vision-Language Model is utilized to generate multi-view hierarchical descriptions across six semantic aspects, providing fine-grained textual guidance. Thirdly, a frequency-guided structure loss is introduced to strengthen the model's sensitivity to high-frequency elements, improving foreground structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DrivePTS achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scenes. Notably, DrivePTS successfully generates rare scenes where prior methods fail, highlighting its strong generalization ability.

</details>


### [16] [Quality-Aware Robust Multi-View Clustering for Heterogeneous Observation Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22568)
*Peihan Wu,Guanjie Cheng,Yufei Tong,Meng Xi,Shuiguang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QARMVC is a novel multi-view clustering framework that addresses heterogeneous observation noise by using an information bottleneck mechanism to quantify fine-grained contamination intensity and derive instance-level quality scores, which guide adaptive noise suppression at the feature level and quality-weighted aggregation at the fusion level.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing noisy robust multi-view clustering methods rely on a simplified binary assumption (data is either perfectly clean or completely corrupted), overlooking the prevalent existence of heterogeneous observation noise where contamination intensity varies continuously across data.

Method: QARMVC employs an information bottleneck mechanism to extract intrinsic semantics for view reconstruction. It uses reconstruction discrepancy to quantify fine-grained contamination intensity and derive instance-level quality scores. These scores guide a hierarchical learning strategy: at the feature level, a quality-weighted contrastive objective suppresses noise propagation; at the fusion level, quality-weighted aggregation constructs a high-quality global consensus, which is then used to align and rectify local views via mutual information maximization.

Result: Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that QARMVC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in scenarios with heterogeneous noise intensities.

Conclusion: The proposed QARMVC framework effectively handles heterogeneous observation noise in multi-view clustering by quantifying noise levels and adaptively adjusting the clustering process based on data quality, bridging the gap between simplified binary noise assumptions and real-world complex noise scenarios.

Abstract: Deep multi-view clustering has achieved remarkable progress but remains vulnerable to complex noise in real-world applications. Existing noisy robust methods predominantly rely on a simplified binary assumption, treating data as either perfectly clean or completely corrupted. This overlooks the prevalent existence of heterogeneous observation noise, where contamination intensity varies continuously across data. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel framework termed Quality-Aware Robust Multi-View Clustering (QARMVC). Specifically, QARMVC employs an information bottleneck mechanism to extract intrinsic semantics for view reconstruction. Leveraging the insight that noise disrupts semantic integrity and impedes reconstruction, we utilize the resulting reconstruction discrepancy to precisely quantify fine-grained contamination intensity and derive instance-level quality scores. These scores are integrated into a hierarchical learning strategy: at the feature level, a quality-weighted contrastive objective is designed to adaptively suppress the propagation of noise; at the fusion level, a high-quality global consensus is constructed via quality-weighted aggregation, which is subsequently utilized to align and rectify local views via mutual information maximization. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that QARMVC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in scenarios with heterogeneous noise intensities.

</details>


### [17] [Guidance Matters: Rethinking the Evaluation Pitfall for Text-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22570)
*Dian Xie,Shitong Shao,Lichen Bai,Zikai Zhou,Bojun Cheng,Shuo Yang,Jun Wu,Zeke Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper reveals critical evaluation pitfalls in diffusion guidance research, showing that common human preference models are biased toward large guidance scales, and proposes a fair evaluation framework (GA-Eval) that demonstrates most advanced guidance methods actually degrade performance compared to standard CFG when properly evaluated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent diffusion guidance methods claim improved generation quality and human preference, but the authors question whether these improvements are truly significant and solid, as there may be evaluation biases undermining valid comparisons.

Method: The authors introduce four contributions: (1) Identifies a critical evaluation pitfall where human preference models are biased toward large guidance scales, (2) Proposes a Guidance-Aware Evaluation (GA-Eval) framework with guidance scale calibration to enable fair comparisons, (3) Designs Transcendent Diffusion Guidance (TDG) that appears superior in conventional evaluation but fails in practice, (4) Empirically evaluates 8 recent methods using both conventional and GA-Eval frameworks.

Result: Simple CFG scaling can compete with most studied guidance methods, but all methods suffer from winning rate degradation over standard CFG. The conventional evaluation framework shows TDG performing better, but GA-Eval reveals it doesn't work in practice. The evaluation bias toward large guidance scales significantly skews quantitative results.

Conclusion: The work reveals that current evaluation paradigms for diffusion guidance are flawed and motivates the community to rethink evaluation methodologies and future research directions in this field.

Abstract: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) has helped diffusion models achieve great conditional generation in various fields. Recently, more diffusion guidance methods have emerged with improved generation quality and human preference. However, can these emerging diffusion guidance methods really achieve solid and significant improvements? In this paper, we rethink recent progress on diffusion guidance. Our work mainly consists of four contributions. First, we reveal a critical evaluation pitfall that common human preference models exhibit a strong bias towards large guidance scales. Simply increasing the CFG scale can easily improve quantitative evaluation scores due to strong semantic alignment, even if image quality is severely damaged (e.g., oversaturation and artifacts). Second, we introduce a novel guidance-aware evaluation (GA-Eval) framework that employs effective guidance scale calibration to enable fair comparison between current guidance methods and CFG by identifying the effects orthogonal and parallel to CFG effects. Third, motivated by the evaluation pitfall, we design Transcendent Diffusion Guidance (TDG) method that can significantly improve human preference scores in the conventional evaluation framework but actually does not work in practice. Fourth, in extensive experiments, we empirically evaluate recent eight diffusion guidance methods within the conventional evaluation framework and the proposed GA-Eval framework. Notably, simply increasing the CFG scales can compete with most studied diffusion guidance methods, while all methods suffer severely from winning rate degradation over standard CFG. Our work would strongly motivate the community to rethink the evaluation paradigm and future directions of this field.

</details>


### [18] [GIFSplat: Generative Prior-Guided Iterative Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22571)
*Tianyu Chen,Wei Xiang,Kang Han,Yu Lu,Di Wu,Gaowen Liu,Ramana Rao Kompella*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GIFSplat introduces a feed-forward iterative refinement framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting from sparse unposed views using forward-only residual updates and frozen diffusion priors, achieving state-of-the-art quality with second-scale inference time across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods are limited by the one-shot prediction paradigm, lack inference-time refinement, struggle with generative priors (especially for out-of-domain data), and cannot maintain efficiency when generative priors are introduced. These limitations stem from strict capacity bounds, inability to continuously inject generative priors, and the trade-off between quality and speed.

Method: GIFSplat consists of two main components: (1) A feed-forward iterative refinement framework that uses a small number of forward-only residual updates to progressively refine the current 3D scene using rendering evidence; (2) A diffusion prior distillation approach that creates Gaussian-level cues from enhanced novel renderings without gradient backpropagation or view-set expansion, enabling per-scene adaptation with generative priors.

Result: GIFSplat consistently outperforms state-of-the-art feed-forward baselines across DL3DV, RealEstate10K, and DTU datasets, improving PSNR by up to +2.1 dB. It maintains second-scale inference time without requiring camera poses or any test-time gradient optimization.

Conclusion: GIFSplat achieves a favorable balance between efficiency and quality by enabling per-scene adaptation with generative priors while preserving feed-forward efficiency. The approach addresses key limitations of existing feed-forward methods through iterative refinement and frozen diffusion prior distillation, making it suitable for real-time applications where both speed and quality are critical.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction offers substantial runtime advantages over per-scene optimization, which remains slow at inference and often fragile under sparse views. However, existing feed-forward methods still have potential for further performance gains, especially for out-of-domain data, and struggle to retain second-level inference time once a generative prior is introduced. These limitations stem from the one-shot prediction paradigm in existing feed-forward pipeline: models are strictly bounded by capacity, lack inference-time refinement, and are ill-suited for continuously injecting generative priors. We introduce GIFSplat, a purely feed-forward iterative refinement framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting from sparse unposed views. A small number of forward-only residual updates progressively refine current 3D scene using rendering evidence, achieve favorable balance between efficiency and quality. Furthermore, we distill a frozen diffusion prior into Gaussian-level cues from enhanced novel renderings without gradient backpropagation or ever-increasing view-set expansion, thereby enabling per-scene adaptation with generative prior while preserving feed-forward efficiency. Across DL3DV, RealEstate10K, and DTU, GIFSplat consistently outperforms state-of-the-art feed-forward baselines, improving PSNR by up to +2.1 dB, and it maintains second-scale inference time without requiring camera poses or any test-time gradient optimization.

</details>


### [19] [Causal Motion Diffusion Models for Autoregressive Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22594)
*Qing Yu,Akihisa Watanabe,Kent Fujiwara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CMDM introduces a unified framework for autoregressive motion generation using a causal diffusion transformer in a semantically aligned latent space, supporting high-quality text-to-motion generation, streaming synthesis, and long-horizon motion at interactive rates with reduced inference latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion generation approaches face limitations: full-sequence diffusion models with bidirectional generation limit temporal causality and real-time applicability, while autoregressive models suffer from instability and cumulative errors.

Method: CMDM builds upon a Motion-Language-Aligned Causal VAE (MAC-VAE) that encodes motion into temporally causal latent representations. An autoregressive diffusion transformer is trained with causal diffusion forcing across motion frames. For fast inference, it employs a frame-wise sampling schedule with causal uncertainty, where each subsequent frame is predicted from partially denoised previous frames.

Result: CMDM outperforms existing diffusion and autoregressive models in semantic fidelity and temporal smoothness on HumanML3D and SnapMoGen datasets, while substantially reducing inference latency. It supports text-to-motion generation, streaming synthesis, and long-horizon motion generation at interactive rates.

Conclusion: CMDM effectively addresses the limitations of existing motion generation approaches by combining causality with diffusion-based quality, demonstrating that a unified framework can balance high-quality motion generation with efficient, real-time performance across diverse motion generation tasks.

Abstract: Recent advances in motion diffusion models have substantially improved the realism of human motion synthesis. However, existing approaches either rely on full-sequence diffusion models with bidirectional generation, which limits temporal causality and real-time applicability, or autoregressive models that suffer from instability and cumulative errors. In this work, we present Causal Motion Diffusion Models (CMDM), a unified framework for autoregressive motion generation based on a causal diffusion transformer that operates in a semantically aligned latent space. CMDM builds upon a Motion-Language-Aligned Causal VAE (MAC-VAE), which encodes motion sequences into temporally causal latent representations. On top of this latent representation, an autoregressive diffusion transformer is trained using causal diffusion forcing to perform temporally ordered denoising across motion frames. To achieve fast inference, we introduce a frame-wise sampling schedule with causal uncertainty, where each subsequent frame is predicted from partially denoised previous frames. The resulting framework supports high-quality text-to-motion generation, streaming synthesis, and long-horizon motion generation at interactive rates. Experiments on HumanML3D and SnapMoGen demonstrate that CMDM outperforms existing diffusion and autoregressive models in both semantic fidelity and temporal smoothness, while substantially reducing inference latency.

</details>


### [20] [Don't let the information slip away](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22595)
*Taozhe Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Association DETR, a novel object detection model that leverages contextual background information (e.g., roads for cars, forests for wild animals) to improve detection performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on the COCO dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing state-of-the-art object detectors (YOLO series, RT-DETR) focus primarily on foreground features while neglecting valuable contextual information from the background. Background context (e.g., cars appearing on roads, wild animals in forests) can significantly aid object detection accuracy.

Method: The authors propose "Association DETR," a detection transformer model that incorporates and leverages contextual information from the background to assist in object detection tasks.

Result: Association DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance on the COCO val2017 dataset, outperforming existing models including YOLOv12 (55.2 mAP) and RT-DETRv2 (53.4 mAP).

Conclusion: Incorporating contextual background information into object detection models can significantly improve detection accuracy, and Association DETR demonstrates this approach achieves SOTA results.

Abstract: Real-time object detection has advanced rapidly in recent years. The YOLO series of detectors is among the most well-known CNN-based object detection models and cannot be overlooked. The latest version, YOLOv26, was recently released, while YOLOv12 achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with 55.2 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. Meanwhile, transformer-based object detection models, also known as DEtection TRansformer (DETR), have demonstrated impressive performance. RT-DETR is an outstanding model that outperformed the YOLO series in both speed and accuracy when it was released. Its successor, RT-DETRv2, achieved 53.4 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. However, despite their remarkable performance, all these models let information to slip away. They primarily focus on the features of foreground objects while neglecting the contextual information provided by the background. We believe that background information can significantly aid object detection tasks. For example, cars are more likely to appear on roads rather than in offices, while wild animals are more likely to be found in forests or remote areas rather than on busy streets. To address this gap, we propose an object detection model called Association DETR, which achieves state-of-the-art results compared to other object detection models on the COCO val2017 dataset.

</details>


### [21] [BetterScene: 3D Scene Synthesis with Representation-Aligned Generative Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22596)
*Yuci Han,Charles Toth,John E. Anderson,William J. Shuart,Alper Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BetterScene enhances novel view synthesis from extremely sparse, unconstrained photos by introducing temporal equivariance regularization and vision foundation model-aligned representation applied to the VAE module in Stable Video Diffusion, integrated with 3D Gaussian Splatting for artifact-free, view-consistent novel views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional diffusion-based NVS methods only fine-tune the UNet module while keeping other components frozen, leading to inconsistent details and artifacts even when incorporating geometry-aware regularizations like depth or semantic conditions.

Method: BetterScene introduces two components applied to the VAE module in the SVD pipeline: (1) temporal equivariance regularization, and (2) vision foundation model-aligned representation. It integrates a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model to render features as inputs for the SVD enhancer.

Result: Evaluated on the challenging DL3DV-10K dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The approach successfully mitigates artifacts and recovers view-consistent details for diverse real-world scenes using extremely sparse, unconstrained photos.

Abstract: We present BetterScene, an approach to enhance novel view synthesis (NVS) quality for diverse real-world scenes using extremely sparse, unconstrained photos. BetterScene leverages the production-ready Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) model pretrained on billions of frames as a strong backbone, aiming to mitigate artifacts and recover view-consistent details at inference time. Conventional methods have developed similar diffusion-based solutions to address these challenges of novel view synthesis. Despite significant improvements, these methods typically rely on off-the-shelf pretrained diffusion priors and fine-tune only the UNet module while keeping other components frozen, which still leads to inconsistent details and artifacts even when incorporating geometry-aware regularizations like depth or semantic conditions. To address this, we investigate the latent space of the diffusion model and introduce two components: (1) temporal equivariance regularization and (2) vision foundation model-aligned representation, both applied to the variational autoencoder (VAE) module within the SVD pipeline. BetterScene integrates a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) model to render features as inputs for the SVD enhancer and generate continuous, artifact-free, consistent novel views. We evaluate on the challenging DL3DV-10K dataset and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [22] [LoR-LUT: Learning Compact 3D Lookup Tables via Low-Rank Residuals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22607)
*Ziqi Zhao,Abhijit Mishra,Shounak Roychowdhury*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LoR-LUT is a unified low-rank formulation for generating compact and interpretable 3D lookup tables (LUTs) for image enhancement by jointly using low-rank residual corrections with basis LUTs, achieving superior perceptual quality with significantly fewer parameters and maintaining trilinear interpolation complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional 3D-LUT-based techniques rely on fusion of dense basis LUTs, which limits compactness and interpretability. The paper seeks to improve upon this by incorporating residual corrections (low-rank tensors) together with basis LUTs, while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: LoR-LUT extends the existing 3D-LUT framework by jointly using low-rank residual corrections with a set of basis LUTs. Unlike conventional approaches that only use dense basis LUTs, this unified formulation leverages the low-rank structure of residual corrections to reduce parameters while preserving trilinear interpolation complexity.

Result: Experimental results on the MIT-Adobe FiveK dataset demonstrate expert-level retouching characteristics with high perceptual fidelity, sub-megabyte model size, and improved perceptual quality compared to conventional methods. Additionally, an interactive visualization tool called LoR-LUT Viewer was introduced to enhance interpretability and user confidence.

Conclusion: The proposed LoR-LUT formulation offers a compact, interpretable, and efficient direction for future LUT-based image enhancement and style transfer applications.

Abstract: We present LoR-LUT, a unified low-rank formulation for compact and interpretable 3D lookup table (LUT) generation. Unlike conventional 3D-LUT-based techniques that rely on fusion of basis LUTs, which are usually dense tensors, our unified approach extends the current framework by jointly using residual corrections, which are in fact low-rank tensors, together with a set of basis LUTs. The approach described here improves the existing perceptual quality of an image, which is primarily due to the technique's novel use of residual corrections. At the same time, we achieve the same level of trilinear interpolation complexity, using a significantly smaller number of network, residual corrections, and LUT parameters. The experimental results obtained from LoR-LUT, which is trained on the MIT-Adobe FiveK dataset, reproduce expert-level retouching characteristics with high perceptual fidelity and a sub-megabyte model size. Furthermore, we introduce an interactive visualization tool, termed LoR-LUT Viewer, which transforms an input image into the LUT-adjusted output image, via a number of slidebars that control different parameters. The tool provides an effective way to enhance interpretability and user confidence in the visual results. Overall, our proposed formulation offers a compact, interpretable, and efficient direction for future LUT-based image enhancement and style transfer.

</details>


### [23] [Spectrally Distilled Representations Aligned with Instruction-Augmented LLMs for Satellite Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22613)
*Minh Kha Do,Wei Xiang,Kang Han,Di Wu,Khoa Phan,Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen,Gaowen Liu,Ramana Rao Kompella*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SATtxt is a spectrum-aware vision-language foundation model that performs inference with RGB-only inputs while retaining spectral cues learned from multi-spectral training data, using two-stage learning: spectral representation distillation from a multi-spectral teacher to an RGB student, and spectrally grounded alignment with instruction-augmented LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLFMs promise zero-shot and retrieval understanding for Earth observation, but their adoption for satellite imagery is hindered by: (1) multi-spectral inputs being informative but difficult to exploit consistently due to band redundancy and misalignment; and (2) CLIP-style text encoders limiting semantic expressiveness and weakening fine-grained alignment. Operational satellite systems often lack full multi-spectral coverage, making RGB-only inference highly desirable for scalable deployment.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) Spectral Representation Distillation - transfers spectral priors from a frozen multi-spectral teacher to an RGB student via a lightweight projector; (2) Spectrally Grounded Alignment with Instruction-Augmented LLMs - bridges the distilled visual space with an expressive LLM embedding space.

Result: Across EuroSAT, BigEarthNet, and ForestNet, SATtxt improves zero-shot classification by 4.2%, retrieval by 5.9%, and linear probing by 2.7% over baselines.

Conclusion: SATtxt demonstrates an efficient path toward spectrum-aware vision-language learning for Earth observation, enabling effective performance with RGB-only inputs at inference while retaining the benefits of multi-spectral training.

Abstract: Vision-language foundation models (VLFMs) promise zero-shot and retrieval understanding for Earth observation. While operational satellite systems often lack full multi-spectral coverage, making RGB-only inference highly desirable for scalable deployment, the adoption of VLFMs for satellite imagery remains hindered by two factors: (1) multi-spectral inputs are informative but difficult to exploit consistently due to band redundancy and misalignment; and (2) CLIP-style text encoders limit semantic expressiveness and weaken fine-grained alignment. We present SATtxt, a spectrum-aware VLFM that operates with RGB inputs only at inference while retaining spectral cues learned during training. Our framework comprises two stages. First, Spectral Representation Distillation transfers spectral priors from a frozen multi-spectral teacher to an RGB student via a lightweight projector. Second, Spectrally Grounded Alignment with Instruction-Augmented LLMs bridges the distilled visual space and an expressive LLM embedding space. Across EuroSAT, BigEarthNet, and ForestNet, SATtxt improves zero-shot classification on average by 4.2%, retrieval by 5.9%, and linear probing by 2.7% over baselines, showing an efficient path toward spectrum-aware vision-language learning for Earth observation. Project page: https://ikhado.github.io/sattxt/

</details>


### [24] [Coded-E2LF: Coded Aperture Light Field Imaging from Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22620)
*Tomoya Tsuchida,Keita Takahashi,Chihiro Tsutake,Toshiaki Fujii,Hajime Nagahara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: We propose Coded-E2LF (coded event to light field), a computational imaging method for acquiring a 4-D light field using a coded aperture and a stationary event-only camera. In a previous work, an imaging system similar to ours was adopted, but both events and intensity images were captured and used for light field reconstruction. In contrast, our method is purely event-based, which relaxes restrictions for hardware implementation. We also introduce several advancements from the previous work that enable us to theoretically support and practically improve light field reconstruction from events alone. In particular, we clarify the key role of a black pattern in aperture coding patterns. We finally implemented our method on real imaging hardware to demonstrate its effectiveness in capturing real 3-D scenes. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to demonstrate that a 4-D light field with pixel-level accuracy can be reconstructed from events alone. Our software and supplementary video are available from our project website.

</details>


### [25] [CGSA: Class-Guided Slot-Aware Adaptation for Source-Free Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22621)
*Boyang Dai,Zeng Fan,Zihao Qi,Meng Lou,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CGSA introduces Object-Centric Learning into Source-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection by using hierarchical slot representations and class-guided contrastive learning to disentangle domain-specific and domain-invariant features, achieving superior performance without source data retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SF-DAOD methods focus on pseudo-label thresholds or teacher-student frameworks but overlook object-level structural cues in cross-domain data, limiting adaptation effectiveness in privacy-sensitive scenarios where source data cannot be retained.

Method: CGSA integrates slot-aware adaptation into DETR-based detectors through two key components: (1) Hierarchical Slot Awareness (HSA) module for progressive disentanglement of images into slot representations as visual priors, and (2) Class-Guided Slot Contrast (CGSC) module that aligns slots with class semantics to maintain semantic consistency.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain datasets demonstrate that CGSA outperforms previous SF-DAOD methods, with theoretical derivations and analysis confirming the effectiveness of both the HSA module and CGSC module.

Conclusion: Object-centric design shows significant promise for privacy-sensitive adaptation scenarios, as CGSA successfully adapts detectors without source data while leveraging object-level structural cues for improved domain adaptation performance.

Abstract: Source-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection (SF-DAOD) aims to adapt a detector trained on a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain without retaining any source data. Despite recent progress, most popular approaches focus on tuning pseudo-label thresholds or refining the teacher-student framework, while overlooking object-level structural cues within cross-domain data. In this work, we present CGSA, the first framework that brings Object-Centric Learning (OCL) into SF-DAOD by integrating slot-aware adaptation into the DETR-based detector. Specifically, our approach integrates a Hierarchical Slot Awareness (HSA) module into the detector to progressively disentangle images into slot representations that act as visual priors. These slots are then guided toward class semantics via a Class-Guided Slot Contrast (CGSC) module, maintaining semantic consistency and prompting domain-invariant adaptation. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms previous SF-DAOD methods, with theoretical derivations and experimental analysis further demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed components and the framework, thereby indicating the promise of object-centric design in privacy-sensitive adaptation scenarios. Code is released at https://github.com/Michael-McQueen/CGSA.

</details>


### [26] [Instruction-based Image Editing with Planning, Reasoning, and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22624)
*Liya Ji,Chenyang Qi,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modality framework that bridges understanding and generation for instruction-based image editing via Chain-of-Thought planning, editing region reasoning, and hint-guided editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction-based image editing is challenging due to high requirements for scene understanding and generation. Prior work using chains of LLMs, segmentation models, and editing models only provides single modality abilities, limiting editing quality.

Method: The proposed method separates the instruction editing task into three components: 1) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) planning where an LLM reasons appropriate sub-prompts based on the instruction and editing network ability; 2) Editing region reasoning where an instruction-based editing region generation network is trained with a multi-modal LLM; 3) Hint-guided instruction-based editing using a text-to-image diffusion model that accepts hints for generation.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method has competitive editing abilities on complex real-world images.

Conclusion: By bridging understanding and generation through multi-modality, the proposed framework effectively handles complex instruction-based image editing tasks.

Abstract: Editing images via instruction provides a natural way to generate interactive content, but it is a big challenge due to the higher requirement of scene understanding and generation. Prior work utilizes a chain of large language models, object segmentation models, and editing models for this task. However, the understanding models provide only a single modality ability, restricting the editing quality.
