Conclusion: The proposed distributed caching approach effectively enhances inference performance by cooperatively sharing intermediate processing states across multiple low-end edge devices while minimizing communication overhead

Abstract: Since local LLM inference on resource-constrained edge devices imposes a severe performance bottleneck, this paper proposes distributed prompt caching to enhance inference performance by cooperatively sharing intermediate processing states across multiple low-end edge devices. To fully utilize prompt similarity, our distributed caching mechanism also supports partial matching. As this approach introduces communication overhead associated with state sharing over a wireless network, we introduce a Bloom-filter-based data structure, referred to as a catalog, to determine whether a remote server possesses the desired internal states, thereby suppressing unnecessary communication. Experiments using the Gemma-3 270M model and the MMLU dataset on the Raspberry Pi Zero 2W platform demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces TTFT (Time to First Token) and TTLT (Time to Last Token) by 93.12% and 50.07% on average, respectively.

</details>


### [283] [Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22817)
*Shuo He,Lang Feng,Qi Wei,Xin Cheng,Lei Feng,Bo An*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HGPO addresses context inconsistency in stepwise group-based RL for long-horizon agentic tasks by assigning steps to hierarchical groups based on historical context consistency and aggregating advantages with adaptive weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Group-based RL (e.g., GRPO) advances LLMs on long-horizon tasks, but stepwise group-based methods suffer from context inconsistency - steps in the same group may have different historical contexts, leading to biased advantage estimation and degraded policy optimization.

Method: Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization (HGPO): Within a group of rollout trajectories, each step is assigned to multiple hierarchical groups based on historical context consistency; distinct advantages are computed within each group and aggregated using an adaptive weighting scheme; no extra models or rollouts required.

Result: Evaluated on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, HGPO significantly outperforms existing agentic RL methods under the same computational constraints.

Conclusion: HGPO achieves a favorable bias-variance trade-off in stepwise advantage estimation without extra models or rollouts, demonstrating its effectiveness for long-horizon agentic tasks.

Abstract: Group-based reinforcement learning (RL), such as GRPO, has advanced the capabilities of large language models on long-horizon agentic tasks. To enable more fine-grained policy updates, recent research has increasingly shifted toward stepwise group-based policy optimization, which treats each step in a rollout trajectory independently while using a memory module to retain historical context. However, we find a key issue in estimating stepwise relative advantages, namely context inconsistency, where steps within the same group may differ in their historical contexts. Empirically, we reveal that this issue can lead to severely biased advantage estimation, thereby degrading policy optimization significantly. To address the issue, in this paper, we propose Hierarchy-of-Groups Policy Optimization (HGPO) for long-horizon agentic tasks. Specifically, within a group of rollout trajectories, HGPO assigns each step to multiple hierarchical groups according to the consistency of historical contexts. Then, for each step, HGPO computes distinct advantages within each group and aggregates them with an adaptive weighting scheme. In this way, HGPO can achieve a favorable bias-variance trade-off in stepwise advantage estimation, without extra models or rollouts. Evaluations on two challenging agentic tasks, ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, show that HGPO significantly outperforms existing agentic RL methods under the same computational constraints. Code is available at https://github.com/langfengQ/verl-agent/tree/master/recipe/hgpo.

</details>


### [284] [Hypernetwork-based approach for grid-independent functional data clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22823)
*Anirudh Thatipelli,Ali Siahkoohi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a resolution-independent framework for functional data clustering using auto-encoding with implicit neural representations, enabling clustering to be performed in a weight space decoupled from sampling grid choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing functional data clustering methods operate on sampled grids, causing cluster assignments to depend on resolution, sampling density, or preprocessing choices rather than on the underlying functions themselves.

Method: The framework uses an auto-encoding architecture where a hypernetwork encoder maps coordinate-value pairs to the weight space of an implicit neural representation (INR) decoder. This representation is compact, decoupled from the sampling grid, and the hypernetwork amortizes weight prediction across the dataset. Clustering is then performed in this weight space using standard algorithms.

Result: Competitive clustering performance is demonstrated that is robust to changes in sampling resolution, including generalization to resolutions not seen during training.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a resolution-independent method for functional data clustering, making cluster assignments independent of discretization choices while maintaining competitive performance.

Abstract: Functional data clustering is concerned with grouping functions that share similar structure, yet most existing methods implicitly operate on sampled grids, causing cluster assignments to depend on resolution, sampling density, or preprocessing choices rather than on the underlying functions themselves. To address this limitation, we introduce a framework that maps discretized function observations -- at arbitrary resolution and on arbitrary grids -- into a fixed-dimensional vector space via an auto-encoding architecture. The encoder is a hypernetwork that maps coordinate-value pairs to the weight space of an implicit neural representation (INR), which serves as the decoder. Because INRs represent functions with very few parameters, this design yields compact representations that are decoupled from the sampling grid, while the hypernetwork amortizes weight prediction across the dataset. Clustering is then performed in this weight space using standard algorithms, making the approach agnostic to both the discretization and the choice of clustering method. By means of synthetic and real-world experiments in high-dimensional settings, we demonstrate competitive clustering performance that is robust to changes in sampling resolution -- including generalization to resolutions not seen during training.

</details>


### [285] [Decentralized Ranking Aggregation: Gossip Algorithms for Borda and Copeland Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22847)
*Anna Van Elst,Kerrian Le Caillec,Igor Colin,Stephan Clémençon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses decentralized ranking aggregation using random gossip communication, providing convergence guarantees for Borda, Copeland, median rank, and local Kemenization methods without central coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While ranking aggregation algorithms exist for centralized settings, decentralized systems (peer-to-peer networks, IoT, multi-agent systems) lack reliable methods for computing consensus rankings. Previous decentralized work focused mainly on arithmetic means, not rankings.

Method: The authors propose a random gossip communication approach enabling autonomous agents to compute global ranking consensus via local interactions without central authority. They provide convergence guarantees with explicit rate bounds for Borda and Copeland methods, and decentralized implementations of median rank rule and local Kemenization.

Result: Rigorous convergence guarantees with explicit rate bounds are proven for Borda and Copeland consensus. Decentralized implementations of median rank and local Kemenization are provided. Extensive empirical evaluations across various network topologies and real/synthetic datasets demonstrate quick and reliable convergence.

Conclusion: The proposed gossip-based decentralized consensus methods for ranking aggregation are both theoretically sound (with proven convergence) and practically effective, achieving fast convergence across diverse network configurations and datasets.

Abstract: The concept of ranking aggregation plays a central role in preference analysis, and numerous algorithms for calculating median rankings, often originating in social choice theory, have been documented in the literature, offering theoretical guarantees in a centralized setting, i.e., when all the ranking data to be aggregated can be brought together in a single computing unit. For many technologies (e.g. peer-to-peer networks, IoT, multi-agent systems), extending the ability to calculate consensus rankings with guarantees in a decentralized setting, i.e., when preference data is initially distributed across a communicating network, remains a major methodological challenge. Indeed, in recent years, the literature on decentralized computation has mainly focused on computing or optimizing statistics such as arithmetic means using gossip algorithms. The purpose of this article is precisely to study how to achieve reliable consensus on collective rankings using classical rules (e.g. Borda, Copeland) in a decentralized setting, thereby raising new questions, robustness to corrupted nodes, and scalability through reduced communication costs in particular. The approach proposed and analyzed here relies on random gossip communication, allowing autonomous agents to compute global ranking consensus using only local interactions, without coordination or central authority.
  We provide rigorous convergence guarantees, including explicit rate bounds, for the Borda and Copeland consensus methods. Beyond these rules, we also provide a decentralized implementation of consensus according to the median rank rule and local Kemenization. Extensive empirical evaluations on various network topologies and real and synthetic ranking datasets demonstrate that our algorithms converge quickly and reliably to the correct ranking aggregation.

</details>


### [286] [MEDNA-DFM: A Dual-View FiLM-MoE Model for Explainable DNA Methylation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22850)
*Yi He,Yina Cao,Jixiu Zhai,Di Wang,Junxiao Kong,Tianchi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces MEDNA-DFM, a deep learning model for DNA methylation identification with explainable mechanisms, demonstrating that conserved intrinsic motifs (e.g., GC content) drive generalization across species and revealing a "sequence-structure synergy" hypothesis for the GAGG core motif and upstream A-tract element.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate computational identification of DNA methylation is essential for understanding epigenetic regulation. While deep learning excels at this binary classification task, its "black-box" nature impedes biological insight, making explainable approaches necessary.

Method: The authors introduced MEDNA-DFM, a high-performance deep learning model for DNA methylation identification, alongside mechanism-inspired signal purification algorithms. The model was designed to capture conserved methylation patterns while maintaining explainability.

Result: MEDNA-DFM effectively captures conserved methylation patterns and achieves robust distinction across diverse species. Validation shows generalization is driven by conserved intrinsic motifs (e.g., GC content) rather than phylogenetic proximity. Motifs extracted with the developed algorithms have significantly higher reliability than prior studies. A "sequence-structure synergy" hypothesis was proposed for the GAGG core motif and upstream A-tract element, validated through in silico mutagenesis showing that ablation of either or both elements significantly degrades model performance.

Conclusion: This work provides a powerful tool for methylation prediction and demonstrates how explainable deep learning can drive both methodological innovation and the generation of biological hypotheses, bridging the gap between computational prediction and biological insight.

Abstract: Accurate computational identification of DNA methylation is essential for understanding epigenetic regulation. Although deep learning excels in this binary classification task, its "black-box" nature impedes biological insight. We address this by introducing a high-performance model MEDNA-DFM, alongside mechanism-inspired signal purification algorithms. Our investigation demonstrates that MEDNA-DFM effectively captures conserved methylation patterns, achieving robust distinction across diverse species. Validation on external independent datasets confirms that the model's generalization is driven by conserved intrinsic motifs (e.g., GC content) rather than phylogenetic proximity. Furthermore, applying our developed algorithms extracted motifs with significantly higher reliability than prior studies. Finally, empirical evidence from a Drosophila 6mA case study prompted us to propose a "sequence-structure synergy" hypothesis, suggesting that the GAGG core motif and an upstream A-tract element function cooperatively. We further validated this hypothesis via in silico mutagenesis, confirming that the ablation of either or both elements significantly degrades the model's recognition capabilities. This work provides a powerful tool for methylation prediction and demonstrates how explainable deep learning can drive both methodological innovation and the generation of biological hypotheses.

</details>


### [287] [Fair feature attribution for multi-output prediction: a Shapley-based perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22882)
*Umberto Biccari,Alain Ibáñez de Opakua,José María Mato,Óscar Millet,Roberto Morales,Enrique Zuazua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides an axiomatic characterization of feature attribution for multi-output predictors within the Shapley framework, proving that SHAP explanations must decompose component-wise across outputs, and that any joint-output attribution rule must relax at least one classical Shapley axiom.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While SHAP explanations are routinely computed independently for each output coordinate in multi-output prediction, the theoretical necessity of this practice has remained unclear. The paper aims to clarify the structural constraints in Shapley-based interpretability, particularly regarding fairness-consistent explanations in multi-output learning.

Method: By extending the classical Shapley axioms (efficiency, symmetry, dummy player, and additivity) to vector-valued cooperative games, the authors establish a rigidity theorem demonstrating that any attribution rule satisfying these axioms must necessarily decompose component-wise across outputs.

Result: The paper proves that any joint-output attribution rule must relax at least one of the classical Shapley axioms. Numerical experiments on a biomedical benchmark demonstrate that multi-output models can achieve computational savings in training and deployment while producing SHAP explanations consistent with the component-wise structure.

Conclusion: This result clarifies the precise scope of fairness-consistent explanations in multi-output learning and identifies a previously unformalized structural constraint in Shapley-based interpretability, validating the practice of computing SHAP explanations independently for each output coordinate.

Abstract: In this article, we provide an axiomatic characterization of feature attribution for multi-output predictors within the Shapley framework. While SHAP explanations are routinely computed independently for each output coordinate, the theoretical necessity of this practice has remained unclear. By extending the classical Shapley axioms to vector-valued cooperative games, we establish a rigidity theorem showing that any attribution rule satisfying efficiency, symmetry, dummy player, and additivity must necessarily decompose component-wise across outputs. Consequently, any joint-output attribution rule must relax at least one of the classical Shapley axioms. This result identifies a previously unformalized structural constraint in Shapley-based interpretability, clarifying the precise scope of fairness-consistent explanations in multi-output learning. Numerical experiments on a biomedical benchmark illustrate that multi-output models can yield computational savings in training and deployment, while producing SHAP explanations that remain fully consistent with the component-wise structure imposed by the Shapley axioms.

</details>


### [288] [A Data-Driven Approach to Support Clinical Renal Replacement Therapy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22902)
*Alice Balboni,Luis Escobar,Andrea Manno,Fabrizio Rossi,Maria Cristina Ruffa,Gianluca Villa,Giordano D'Aloisio,Antonio Consolo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops interpretable machine learning models using tabular data to predict membrane fouling in critically ill patients on CRRT, outperforming LSTM models while maintaining high sensitivity (77.6%) and specificity (96.3%) through feature selection and Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Membrane fouling during Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) poses significant clinical challenges by potentially compromising treatment efficacy and patient outcomes. The researchers aimed to develop a reliable, interpretable machine learning approach for early prediction of membrane fouling to enable timely therapeutic interventions and improve patient management in critically ill populations.

Method: The researchers employed a tabular data approach using 16 clinically selected features from ICU time-series data rather than direct temporal modeling. To address class imbalance, they applied ADASYN oversampling. They tested Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM algorithms, performed feature selection to reduce to five key variables, and used Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis to interpret model predictions.

Result: The models achieved balanced performance with 77.6% sensitivity and 96.3% specificity at a 10% rebalancing rate. Performance remained robust across different forecasting horizons. The tabular approach significantly outperformed LSTM recurrent neural networks. Feature selection to five key variables maintained accuracy while improving model simplicity. Counterfactual analysis successfully identified minimal input changes capable of reversing fouling predictions.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the viability of interpretable machine learning models for predicting membrane fouling during CRRT. The integration of prediction with counterfactual analysis provides practical clinical value by identifying minimal input changes that could reverse fouling predictions, potentially guiding therapeutic adjustments to reduce fouling risk and improve patient management in intensive care settings.

Abstract: This study investigates a data-driven machine learning approach to predict membrane fouling in critically ill patients undergoing Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT). Using time-series data from an ICU, 16 clinically selected features were identified to train predictive models. To ensure interpretability and enable reliable counterfactual analysis, the researchers adopted a tabular data approach rather than modeling temporal dependencies directly. Given the imbalance between fouling and non-fouling cases, the ADASYN oversampling technique was applied to improve minority class representation. Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM models were tested, achieving balanced performance with 77.6% sensitivity and 96.3% specificity at a 10% rebalancing rate. Results remained robust across different forecasting horizons. Notably, the tabular approach outperformed LSTM recurrent neural networks, suggesting that explicit temporal modeling was not necessary for strong predictive performance. Feature selection further reduced the model to five key variables, improving simplicity and interpretability with minimal loss of accuracy. A Shapley value-based counterfactual analysis was applied to the best-performing model, successfully identifying minimal input changes capable of reversing fouling predictions. Overall, the findings support the viability of interpretable machine learning models for predicting membrane fouling during CRRT. The integration of prediction and counterfactual analysis offers practical clinical value, potentially guiding therapeutic adjustments to reduce fouling risk and improve patient management.

</details>


### [289] [Generalization Bounds of Stochastic Gradient Descent in Homogeneous Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22936)
*Wenquan Ma,Yang Sui,Jiaye Teng,Bohan Wang,Jing Xu,Jingqin Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that homogeneous neural networks can utilize slower stepsize decay (Ω(1/√t) instead of O(1/t)) while maintaining generalization bounds, improving optimization performance in practical scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Algorithmic stability is crucial for generalization analysis, but current stability theory requires rigid stepsize decay η_t = O(1/t) under non-convex training regimes. This decay rate may impede optimization and doesn't align with practical training scenarios, necessitating conditions that allow slower stepsize decay.

Method: The authors derive generalization bounds under homogeneous neural network regimes, proving that this regime enables slower stepsize decay of order Ω(1/√t) under mild assumptions. They further extend theoretical results to non-Lipschitz regimes.

Result: Homogeneous neural networks (including fully-connected and convolutional networks with ReLU and LeakyReLU activations) allow for stepsize decay of order Ω(1/√t) while maintaining generalization guarantees. This slower decay rate applies under mild assumptions and extends to non-Lipschitz regimes.

Conclusion: This finding is broadly applicable to common neural network architectures and enables better optimization performance by allowing more practical stepsize decay rates than the standard O(1/t) requirement. The results provide more aligned theoretical foundations with practical training scenarios.

Abstract: Algorithmic stability is among the most potent techniques in generalization analysis. However, its derivation usually requires a stepsize $η_t = \mathcal{O}(1/t)$ under non-convex training regimes, where $t$ denotes iterations. This rigid decay of the stepsize potentially impedes optimization and may not align with practical scenarios. In this paper, we derive the generalization bounds under the homogeneous neural network regimes, proving that this regime enables slower stepsize decay of order $Ω(1/\sqrt{t})$ under mild assumptions. We further extend the theoretical results from several aspects, e.g., non-Lipschitz regimes. This finding is broadly applicable, as homogeneous neural networks encompass fully-connected and convolutional neural networks with ReLU and LeakyReLU activations.

</details>


### [290] [MSINO: Curvature-Aware Sobolev Optimization for Manifold Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22937)
*Suresan Pareth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSINO introduces a curvature-aware training framework for Riemannian manifold neural networks using covariant Sobolev loss with parallel transport and Laplace Beltrami regularization, providing theoretical convergence guarantees including a manifold Descent Lemma, Sobolev Polyak-Łojasiewicz inequality, and Newton-Sobolev method with quadratic contraction in curvature-controlled neighborhoods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural network training lacks proper handling of curvature in Riemannian manifold settings (e.g., robotics on SO(3)/SE(3), physics-informed learning). Prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space cannot provide curvature-dependent training time guarantees or properly account for geometry through parallel transport, limiting theoretical rigor and practical performance on manifold-structured problems.

Method: MSINO replaces Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients via parallel transport and includes Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization. The framework derives geometry-dependent constants yielding: (1) a Descent Lemma with manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (2) a Sobolev Polyak-Łojasiewicz inequality for linear convergence under step size bounds, and (3) a two-step Newton-Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature-controlled neighborhoods.

Result: The framework provides explicit training time guarantees that track curvature and transported Jacobians. Unlike prior Euclidean Sobolev approaches, MSINO achieves provable convergence rates for Riemannian gradient descent and SGD on manifolds. The method unifies value-based and gradient-based learning with rigorous curvature-aware convergence analysis applicable to surface imaging, physics-informed learning, and robotics on Lie groups (SO(3), SE(3)).

Conclusion: MSINO establishes a principled theoretical foundation for training neural networks on Riemannian manifolds by incorporating curvature information through Sobolev theory and parallel transport. The framework delivers both practical benefits (improved stability via smoothness regularization) and theoretical guarantees (linear convergence, quadratic Newton-Sobolev contraction), enabling robust learning in geometric domains that were previously underserved by standard Euclidean training approaches.

Abstract: We introduce Manifold Sobolev Informed Neural Optimization (MSINO), a curvature aware training framework for neural networks defined on Riemannian manifolds. The method replaces standard Euclidean derivative supervision with a covariant Sobolev loss that aligns gradients using parallel transport and improves stability via a Laplace Beltrami smoothness regularization term.
  Building on classical results in Riemannian optimization and Sobolev theory on manifolds, we derive geometry dependent constants that yield (i) a Descent Lemma with a manifold Sobolev smoothness constant, (ii) a Sobolev Polyak Lojasiewicz inequality giving linear convergence guarantees for Riemannian gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent under explicit step size bounds, and (iii) a two step Newton Sobolev method with local quadratic contraction in curvature controlled neighborhoods.
  Unlike prior Sobolev training in Euclidean space, MSINO provides training time guarantees that explicitly track curvature and transported Jacobians. Applications include surface imaging, physics informed learning settings, and robotics on Lie groups such as SO(3) and SE(3). The framework unifies value and gradient based learning with curvature aware convergence guarantees for neural training on manifolds.

</details>


### [291] [Scaling Laws of Global Weather Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22962)
*Yuejiang Yu,Langwen Huang,Alexandru Calotoiu,Torsten Hoefler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes empirical scaling laws in weather forecasting models, investigating how model performance depends on model size, dataset size, and compute budget. It finds that Aurora exhibits strong data-scaling behavior, GraphCast is parameter-efficient but has limited hardware utilization, and weather models consistently favor wider over deeper architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven models are revolutionizing weather forecasting, but there is a need to optimize training efficiency and model performance.

Method: The study investigates the relationship between model performance (validation loss) and three key factors: model size (N), dataset size (D), and compute budget (C). It analyzes various models across a range, performs compute-optimal analysis, and examines model shape (width vs depth) to uncover scaling behaviors.

Result: Aurora demonstrates the strongest data-scaling behavior (10x training data reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x), GraphCast shows the highest parameter efficiency but suffers from limited hardware utilization, and compute-optimal analysis reveals that longer training durations yield greater performance gains than increasing model size under fixed compute budgets. Weather forecasting models consistently favor increased width over depth.

Conclusion: Future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

Abstract: Data-driven models are revolutionizing weather forecasting. To optimize training efficiency and model performance, this paper analyzes empirical scaling laws within this domain. We investigate the relationship between model performance (validation loss) and three key factors: model size ($N$), dataset size ($D$), and compute budget ($C$). Across a range of models, we find that Aurora exhibits the strongest data-scaling behavior: increasing the training dataset by 10x reduces validation loss by up to 3.2x. GraphCast demonstrates the highest parameter efficiency, yet suffers from limited hardware utilization. Our compute-optimal analysis indicates that, under fixed compute budgets, allocating resources to longer training durations yields greater performance gains than increasing model size. Furthermore, we analyze model shape and uncover scaling behaviors that differ fundamentally from those observed in language models: weather forecasting models consistently favor increased width over depth. These findings suggest that future weather models should prioritize wider architectures and larger effective training datasets to maximize predictive performance.

</details>


### [292] [Exploratory Memory-Augmented LLM Agent via Hybrid On- and Off-Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23008)
*Zeyuan Liu,Jeonghye Kim,Xufang Luo,Dongsheng Li,Yuqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMPO² is a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on/off-policy updates, achieving 128.6% and 11.3% improvements over GRPO on ScienceWorld and WebShop respectively, while demonstrating superior adaptability to new tasks with minimal trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploration remains a key bottleneck for large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning. Prior methods exploiting pretrained knowledge fail in environments requiring discovery of novel states.

Method: EMPO² (Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization) is a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on-policy and off-policy updates, enabling LLMs to perform well with memory while maintaining robustness without it.

Result: On ScienceWorld: 128.6% improvement over GRPO; on WebShop: 11.3% improvement over GRPO; superior adaptability to new tasks in out-of-distribution tests, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates.

Conclusion: EMPO² demonstrates superior exploratory capabilities and generalizability compared to baseline methods, making it a promising framework for building more effective and adaptable LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Exploration remains the key bottleneck for large language model agents trained with reinforcement learning. While prior methods exploit pretrained knowledge, they fail in environments requiring the discovery of novel states. We propose Exploratory Memory-Augmented On- and Off-Policy Optimization (EMPO$^2$), a hybrid RL framework that leverages memory for exploration and combines on- and off-policy updates to make LLMs perform well with memory while also ensuring robustness without it. On ScienceWorld and WebShop, EMPO$^2$ achieves 128.6% and 11.3% improvements over GRPO, respectively. Moreover, in out-of-distribution tests, EMPO$^2$ demonstrates superior adaptability to new tasks, requiring only a few trials with memory and no parameter updates. These results highlight EMPO$^2$ as a promising framework for building more exploratory and generalizable LLM-based agents.

</details>


### [293] [Learning Disease-Sensitive Latent Interaction Graphs From Noisy Cardiac Flow Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23035)
*Viraj Patel,Marko Grujic,Philipp Aigner,Theodor Abart,Marcus Granegger,Deblina Bhattacharjee,Katharine Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physics-informed latent relational framework that models cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph, combining neural relational inference with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics. The method was applied to aortic coarctation and left ventricular assist device datasets, demonstrating that graph entropy correlates strongly with disease severity and captures structural changes in cardiac blood flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current imaging and computational methods fail to capture the underlying relational structures of coherent flow features in cardiac blood flow patterns, despite these patterns containing rich information about disease severity and clinical interventions.

Method: A physics-informed latent relational framework is proposed, modeling cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph. The model combines a neural relational inference architecture with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics. The approach was applied to computational fluid dynamics simulations of aortic coarctation and ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under varying left ventricular assist device support levels.

Result: In aortic coarctation simulations, vortex interactions become stronger and more frequent as aortic radius narrows, leading to higher graph entropy that correlates monotonically with coarctation severity (R²=0.78, Spearman |ρ|=0.96). In left ventricular assist device datasets, the latent graph representation captures weakening coherent vortical structures, demonstrating cross-modal generalization from simulations to ultrasound imaging.

Conclusion: Latent interaction graphs and entropy serve as robust and interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention levels, with demonstrated cross-modal generalization capabilities between different imaging modalities and disease contexts.

Abstract: Cardiac blood flow patterns contain rich information about disease severity and clinical interventions, yet current imaging and computational methods fail to capture underlying relational structures of coherent flow features. We propose a physics-informed, latent relational framework to model cardiac vortices as interacting nodes in a graph. Our model combines a neural relational inference architecture with physics-inspired interaction energy and birth-death dynamics, yielding a latent graph sensitive to disease severity and intervention level. We first apply this to computational fluid dynamics simulations of aortic coarctation. Learned latent graphs reveal that as the aortic radius narrows, vortex interactions become stronger and more frequent. This leads to a higher graph entropy, correlating monotonically with coarctation severity ($R^2=0.78$, Spearman $|ρ|=0.96$). We then extend this method to ultrasound datasets of left ventricles under varying levels of left ventricular assist device support. Again the latent graph representation captures the weakening of coherent vortical structures, thereby demonstrating cross-modal generalisation. Results show latent interaction graphs and entropy serve as robust and interpretable markers of cardiac disease and intervention.

</details>


### [294] [RhythmBERT: A Self-Supervised Language Model Based on Latent Representations of ECG Waveforms for Heart Disease Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23060)
*Xin Wang,Burcu Ozek,Aruna Mohan,Amirhossein Ravari,Or Zilbershot,Fatemeh Afghah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RhythmBERT is a generative ECG language model that treats cardiac signals as structured language by encoding rhythm segments into symbolic tokens while preserving morphology, pretrained on ~800,000 unlabeled recordings to achieve strong performance on heart disease diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-supervised methods treat ECG as generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Contrastive methods use augmentations that distort morphology, while generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation that misaligns cardiac cycles.

Method: RhythmBERT encodes P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations, combining discrete tokens (capturing rhythm semantics) with continuous embeddings (retaining fine-grained morphology). Pretrained on ~800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings using a masked prediction objective.

Result: Despite using only a single lead, RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines, generalizing from common conditions (atrial fibrillation) to clinically challenging cases (subtle ST-T abnormalities, myocardial infarction).

Conclusion: Considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) analysis is crucial for diagnosing heart disease, but most self-supervised learning methods treat ECG as a generic time series, overlooking physiologic semantics and rhythm-level structure. Existing contrastive methods utilize augmentations that distort morphology, whereas generative approaches employ fixed-window segmentation, which misaligns cardiac cycles. To address these limitations, we propose RhythmBERT, a generative ECG language model that considers ECG as a language paradigm by encoding P, QRS, and T segments into symbolic tokens via autoencoder-based latent representations. These discrete tokens capture rhythm semantics, while complementary continuous embeddings retain fine-grained morphology, enabling a unified view of waveform structure and rhythm. RhythmBERT is pretrained on approximately 800,000 unlabeled ECG recordings with a masked prediction objective, allowing it to learn contextual representations in a label-efficient manner. Evaluations show that despite using only a single lead, RhythmBERT achieves comparable or superior performance to strong 12-lead baselines. This generalization extends from prevalent conditions such as atrial fibrillation to clinically challenging cases such as subtle ST-T abnormalities and myocardial infarction. Our results suggest that considering ECG as structured language offers a scalable and physiologically aligned pathway for advancing cardiac analysis.

</details>


### [295] [Physics-informed neural particle flow for the Bayesian update step](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23089)
*Domonkos Csuzdi,Tamás Bécsi,Olivér Törő*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physics-informed neural particle flow for Bayesian update in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation, which uses a neural network to approximate transport velocity field with a master PDE constraint, enabling unsupervised training and better mode coverage than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Bayesian update step poses significant computational challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation. Existing methods either yield stiff differential equations (log-homotopy particle flow filters) or treat the update as a black-box task/rely on asymptotic relaxation (deep learning approximations), neglecting the exact geometric structure of finite-horizon probability transport.

Method: The authors propose a physics-informed neural particle flow, an amortized inference framework that couples the log-homotopy trajectory of the prior to posterior density function with the continuity equation describing density evolution, yielding a master PDE. This PDE is embedded as a physical constraint into the loss function to train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field.

Result: The approach enables purely unsupervised training, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples. The neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating numerical stiffness inherent to analytic flows and reducing online computational complexity.

Conclusion: Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario confirms better mode coverage and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

Abstract: The Bayesian update step poses significant computational challenges in high-dimensional nonlinear estimation. While log-homotopy particle flow filters offer an alternative to stochastic sampling, existing formulations usually yield stiff differential equations. Conversely, existing deep learning approximations typically treat the update as a black-box task or rely on asymptotic relaxation, neglecting the exact geometric structure of the finite-horizon probability transport. In this work, we propose a physics-informed neural particle flow, which is an amortized inference framework. To construct the flow, we couple the log-homotopy trajectory of the prior to posterior density function with the continuity equation describing the density evolution. This derivation yields a governing partial differential equation (PDE), referred to as the master PDE. By embedding this PDE as a physical constraint into the loss function, we train a neural network to approximate the transport velocity field. This approach enables purely unsupervised training, eliminating the need for ground-truth posterior samples. We demonstrate that the neural parameterization acts as an implicit regularizer, mitigating the numerical stiffness inherent to analytic flows and reducing online computational complexity. Experimental validation on multimodal benchmarks and a challenging nonlinear scenario confirms better mode coverage and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [296] [PRAC: Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression and Memory-Efficient Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23111)
*Yanyi Li,Yimu Zhang,Cong Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRAC (Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression) decomposes activations into a principal subspace (via SVD) and a random subspace, achieving up to 36% memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activations have become the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. Existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, resulting in slow convergence or limited compression.

Method: PRAC novelly decomposes activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. By introducing a precise scaling factor, PRAC yields an unbiased gradient estimator with minimum variance under certain conditions.

Result: Extensive experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks demonstrate that PRAC achieves up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

Conclusion: PRAC effectively addresses the activation memory bottleneck in LLM training by leveraging spectral structure, providing up to 36% memory reduction without significant performance loss, making it suitable for large-batch training scenarios.

Abstract: Activations have become the primary memory bottleneck in large-batch LLM training. However, existing compression methods fail to exploit the spectral structure of activations, resulting in slow convergence or limited compression. To address this, we bridge the relationship between the algorithm's fast convergence and the requirements for subspace projection, and show that an effective compression should yield an unbiased estimate of the original activation with low variance. We propose Principal-Random Subspace for LLM Activation Compression (PRAC), which novelly decomposes activations into two components: a principal subspace captured via SVD to retain dominant information, and a random subspace sampled from the orthogonal complement to approximate the tail. By introducing a precise scaling factor, we prove that PRAC yields an unbiased gradient estimator with minimum variance under certain conditions. Extensive experiments on pre-training and fine-tuning tasks demonstrate that PRAC achieves up to 36% total memory reduction with negligible performance degradation and minimal computational cost.

</details>


### [297] [Learning Physical Operators using Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23113)
*Vignesh Gopakumar,Ander Gray,Dan Giles,Lorenzo Zanisi,Matt J. Kusner,Timo Betcke,Stanislas Pamela,Marc Peter Deisenroth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed training framework that decomposes PDEs using operator splitting, trains separate neural operators for non-linear parts with fixed finite-difference convolutions for linear parts, and formulates the model as a neural ODE for continuous-in-time predictions, achieving superior generalisation and parameter efficiency on Navier-Stokes equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators as surrogate models for PDEs struggle with generalisation beyond training distributions and are constrained to fixed temporal discretisation, limiting their applicability to novel physical regimes and long-term predictions.

Method: The authors introduce a modular mixture-of-experts architecture that decomposes PDEs using operator splitting methods, training separate neural operators to learn individual non-linear physical operators while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions. The modeling task is formulated as a neural ordinary differential equation where learned operators constitute the right-hand side, enabling continuous-in-time predictions through standard ODE solvers.

Result: Demonstrated on incompressible and compressible Navier-Stokes equations, the approach achieves better convergence and superior performance when generalising to unseen physics regimes, while remaining parameter-efficient and enabling temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons.

Conclusion: This physics-informed framework addresses key limitations of neural operators by explicitly encoding underlying operator structure, providing interpretable components that can be verified against known physics, and supporting generalisation to novel physical regimes with continuous-time predictions.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as promising surrogate models for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), but struggle to generalise beyond training distributions and are often constrained to a fixed temporal discretisation. This work introduces a physics-informed training framework that addresses these limitations by decomposing PDEs using operator splitting methods, training separate neural operators to learn individual non-linear physical operators while approximating linear operators with fixed finite-difference convolutions. This modular mixture-of-experts architecture enables generalisation to novel physical regimes by explicitly encoding the underlying operator structure. We formulate the modelling task as a neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) where these learned operators constitute the right-hand side, enabling continuous-in-time predictions through standard ODE solvers and implicitly enforcing PDE constraints. Demonstrated on incompressible and compressible Navier-Stokes equations, our approach achieves better convergence and superior performance when generalising to unseen physics. The method remains parameter-efficient, enabling temporal extrapolation beyond training horizons, and provides interpretable components whose behaviour can be verified against known physics.

</details>


### [298] [Regularized Online RLHF with Generalized Bilinear Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23116)
*Junghyun Lee,Minju Hong,Kwang-Sung Jun,Chulhee Yun,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies contextual online RLHF with general preferences using the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) to capture intransitive preferences, establishing polylogarithmic regret for greedy sampling and the first statistically efficient high-dimensional guarantee via Explore-Then-Commit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to identify the Nash Equilibrium in contextual online RLHF with general preferences. Prior works were limited to reverse KL-regularization, and the authors aim to generalize to any strongly convex regularizer while capturing potentially intransitive preferences.

Method: They adopt the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) with low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices to capture intransitive preferences. The key theoretical contribution is proving that the dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of the estimation error, derived solely from strong convexity and skew-symmetricity. They establish two algorithms: Greedy Sampling and Explore-Then-Commit, with the latter exploiting the low-rank structure.

Result: Two regret bounds are established: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, $e^{O(η)}$-free regret $\tilde{O}(ηd^4 (\log T)^2)$. (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves $\mathrm{poly}(d)$-free regret $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{ηr T})$, which is the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high-dimensions.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high dimensions through the Explore-Then-Commit algorithm, demonstrating that low-rank structure in preferences can be exploited for efficient learning.

Abstract: We consider the problem of contextual online RLHF with general preferences, where the goal is to identify the Nash Equilibrium. We adopt the Generalized Bilinear Preference Model (GBPM) to capture potentially intransitive preferences via low-rank, skew-symmetric matrices. We investigate general preference learning with any strongly convex regularizer (where $η^{-1}$ is the regularization strength), generalizing beyond prior works limited to reverse KL-regularization. Central to our analysis is proving that the dual gap of the greedy policy is bounded by the square of the estimation error - a result derived solely from strong convexity and the skew-symmetricity of GBPM.Building on this insight and a feature diversity assumption, we establish two regret bounds via two simple algorithms: (1) Greedy Sampling achieves polylogarithmic, $e^{O(η)}$-free regret $\tilde{O}(ηd^4 (\log T)^2)$. (2) Explore-Then-Commit achieves $\mathrm{poly}(d)$-free regret $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{ηr T})$ by exploiting the low-rank structure; this is the first statistically efficient guarantee for online RLHF in high-dimensions.

</details>


### [299] [Bound to Disagree: Generalization Bounds via Certifiable Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23128)
*Mathieu Bazinet,Valentina Zantedeschi,Pascal Germain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A disagreement-based framework for deriving computable, tight generalization bounds for deep learning models without modifying the target model or training procedure, validated across sample compression, model compression, and PAC-Bayes approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalization bounds for deep learning are typically vacuous (too loose), non-computable, or limited to specific model classes, making it difficult to quantify the true risk of trained models.

Method: Introduces disagreement-based certificates for the risk gap between any two predictors. Uses a surrogate model with tight generalization guarantees to bound the target predictor's true risk, evaluated on unlabeled data. Three surrogate frameworks are employed: sample compression, model compression, and PAC-Bayes theory. The approach requires no modification of the target model or training procedure.

Result: Empirically demonstrates the tightness of the obtained certificates and showcases the versatility of the approach across different surrogate model frameworks.

Conclusion: Provides a practical, framework-agnostic method for deriving meaningful generalization guarantees for deep learning models, with the important advantage that the target model and its training process remain completely unchanged.

Abstract: Generalization bounds for deep learning models are typically vacuous, not computable or restricted to specific model classes. In this paper, we tackle these issues by providing new disagreement-based certificates for the gap between the true risk of any two predictors. We then bound the true risk of the predictor of interest via a surrogate model that enjoys tight generalization guarantees, and evaluating our disagreement bound on an unlabeled dataset. We empirically demonstrate the tightness of the obtained certificates and showcase the versatility of the approach by training surrogate models leveraging three different frameworks: sample compression, model compression and PAC-Bayes theory. Importantly, such guarantees are achieved without modifying the target model, nor adapting the training procedure to the generalization framework.

</details>


### [300] [DyGnROLE: Modeling Asymmetry in Dynamic Graphs with Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23135)
*Tyler Bonnet,Marek Rei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DyGnROLE introduces a transformer-based architecture for dynamic directed graphs that explicitly disentangles source and destination node representations through separate embeddings and role-semantic positional encodings, enhanced by a self-supervised Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP) pretraining objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic graph architectures rely on shared parameters for processing source and destination nodes, lacking systematic role-aware modeling despite real-world directed graphs exhibiting asymmetrical behavioral patterns and temporal dynamics between source and destination nodes.

Method: DyGnROLE employs a transformer-based architecture that explicitly disentangles source and destination representations using separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings. The model incorporates a self-supervised pretraining objective called Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP) that leverages the full unlabeled interaction history to encode informative structural biases, enabling role-specific representation learning without requiring annotated data.

Result: Evaluation on future edge classification demonstrates that DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, establishing role-aware modeling as an effective strategy for dynamic graph learning.

Conclusion: The explicit disentanglement of source and destination representations through role-aware modeling, combined with the self-supervised TCLP pretraining objective, provides a robust approach for learning from dynamic directed graphs, particularly in low-label regimes where the pretraining objective is crucial for capturing role-specific structural and temporal contexts.

Abstract: Real-world dynamic graphs are often directed, with source and destination nodes exhibiting asymmetrical behavioral patterns and temporal dynamics. However, existing dynamic graph architectures largely rely on shared parameters for processing source and destination nodes, with limited or no systematic role-aware modeling. We propose DyGnROLE (Dynamic Graph Node-Role-Oriented Latent Encoding), a transformer-based architecture that explicitly disentangles source and destination representations. By using separate embedding vocabularies and role-semantic positional encodings, the model captures the distinct structural and temporal contexts unique to each role. Critical to the effectiveness of these specialized embeddings in low-label regimes is a self-supervised pretraining objective we introduce: Temporal Contrastive Link Prediction (TCLP). The pretraining uses the full unlabeled interaction history to encode informative structural biases, enabling the model to learn role-specific representations without requiring annotated data. Evaluation on future edge classification demonstrates that DyGnROLE substantially outperforms a diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines, establishing role-aware modeling as an effective strategy for dynamic graph learning.

</details>


### [301] [Prediction of Diffusion Coefficients in Mixtures with Tensor Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23142)
*Zeno Romero,Kerstin Münnemann,Hans Hasse,Fabian Jirasek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid tensor completion method (TCM) using Tucker decomposition and Bayesian training with prior knowledge from the SEGWE model, combined with active learning for targeted PFG NMR data acquisition, predicts temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures with markedly improved accuracy across 268-378 K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting diffusion coefficients is crucial but experimental data are scarce. Matrix completion methods (MCMs) are effective but restricted to single-temperature predictions, and their accuracy depends strongly on the availability of high-quality experimental data for each temperature of interest.

Method: The TCM employs Tucker decomposition and is jointly trained on experimental data for diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary systems at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. The semi-empirical SEGWE model serves as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. Active learning (AL) strategies are used for targeted acquisition of new diffusion data by pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR measurements.

Result: The TCM extrapolates linearly to any temperature between 268 K and 378 K, achieving markedly improved prediction accuracy compared to established models across all studied temperatures. Incorporating experimental data from 19 solute + solvent systems yields substantial improvement in predictive accuracy.

Conclusion: These findings highlight the potential of combining data-efficient ML methods with adaptive experimentation to advance predictive modeling of transport properties.

Abstract: Predicting diffusion coefficients in mixtures is crucial for many applications, as experimental data remain scarce, and machine learning (ML) offers promising alternatives to established semi-empirical models. Among ML models, matrix completion methods (MCMs) have proven effective in predicting thermophysical properties, including diffusion coefficients in binary mixtures. However, MCMs are restricted to single-temperature predictions, and their accuracy depends strongly on the availability of high-quality experimental data for each temperature of interest. In this work, we address this challenge by presenting a hybrid tensor completion method (TCM) for predicting temperature-dependent diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary mixtures. The TCM employs a Tucker decomposition and is jointly trained on experimental data for diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in binary systems at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Predictions from the semi-empirical SEGWE model serve as prior knowledge within a Bayesian training framework. The TCM then extrapolates linearly to any temperature between 268 K and 378 K, achieving markedly improved prediction accuracy compared to established models across all studied temperatures. To further enhance predictive performance, the experimental database was expanded using active learning (AL) strategies for targeted acquisition of new diffusion data by pulsed-field gradient (PFG) NMR measurements. Diffusion coefficients at infinite dilution in 19 solute + solvent systems were measured at 298 K, 313 K, and 333 K. Incorporating these results yields a substantial improvement in the TCM's predictive accuracy. These findings highlight the potential of combining data-efficient ML methods with adaptive experimentation to advance predictive modeling of transport properties.

</details>


### [302] [Partial recovery of meter-scale surface weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23146)
*Jonathan Giezendanner,Qidong Yang,Eric Schmitt,Anirban Chandra,Daniel Salles Civitarese,Johannes Jakubik,Jeremy Vila,Detlef Hohl,Campbell Watson,Sherrie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates that meter-scale near-surface weather variability (wind, temperature, humidity at 10 m resolution) can be statistically recovered by conditioning coarse atmospheric states on sparse surface measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, reducing wind error by 29% and temperature/dewpoint error by 6% compared to ERA5 while revealing physically interpretable structures like urban heat islands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Near-surface atmospheric conditions vary sharply over tens to hundreds of meters due to land cover and topography, yet this variability is absent from current weather analyses and forecasts. It is unclear whether such meter-scale variability reflects irreducibly chaotic dynamics or contains a component predictable from surface characteristics and large-scale atmospheric forcing.

Method: By conditioning coarse atmospheric states on sparse surface station measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, the authors infer spatially continuous fields of near-surface wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States.

Result: The inferred fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature and dewpoint error by 6% relative to ERA5, while explaining substantially more spatial variance at fixed time steps. The fields exhibit physically interpretable structure, including urban heat islands, evapotranspiration-driven humidity contrasts, and wind speed differences across land cover types.

Conclusion: The findings expand the frontier of weather modeling by demonstrating a computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference. More broadly, they illustrate how conditioning coarse dynamical models on static fine-scale features can reveal previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

Abstract: Near-surface atmospheric conditions can differ sharply over tens to hundreds of meters due to land cover and topography, yet this variability is absent from current weather analyses and forecasts. It is unclear whether such meter-scale variability reflects irreducibly chaotic dynamics or contains a component predictable from surface characteristics and large-scale atmospheric forcing. Here we show that a substantial, physically coherent component of meter-scale near-surface weather is statistically recoverable from existing observations. By conditioning coarse atmospheric state on sparse surface station measurements and high-resolution Earth observation data, we infer spatially continuous fields of near-surface wind, temperature, and humidity at 10 m resolution across the contiguous United States. Relative to ERA5, the inferred fields reduce wind error by 29% and temperature and dewpoint error by 6%, while explaining substantially more spatial variance at fixed time steps. They also exhibit physically interpretable structure, including urban heat islands, evapotranspiration-driven humidity contrasts, and wind speed differences across land cover types. Our findings expand the frontier of weather modeling by demonstrating a computationally feasible approach to continental-scale meter-resolution inference. More broadly, they illustrate how conditioning coarse dynamical models on static fine-scale features can reveal previously unresolved components of the Earth system.

</details>


### [303] [Benchmarking Temporal Web3 Intelligence: Lessons from the FinSurvival 2025 Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23159)
*Oshani Seneviratne,Fernando Spadea,Adrien Pavao,Aaron Micah Green,Kristin P. Bennett*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FinSurvival Challenge 2025 establishes a benchmark for temporal Web3 survival prediction using 21.8M Aave v3 transactions across 16 tasks, demonstrating that domain-aware temporal feature construction outperforms generic approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal Web3 analytics lacks shared, reproducible benchmarks capturing real-world temporal dynamics like censoring and non-stationarity, hindering methodological progress and technique transfer between Web3 and broader Web domains.

Method: Operationalized 16 survival prediction tasks using 21.8 million transaction records from the Aave v3 protocol, designed a comprehensive benchmark, and evaluated both domain-aware temporal feature construction and generic modeling approaches.

Result: Domain-aware temporal feature construction significantly outperformed generic modeling approaches in the survival prediction tasks, demonstrating the importance of domain-specific feature engineering for temporal Web3 intelligence.

Conclusion: Web3 systems provide a high-fidelity sandbox for studying fundamental temporal challenges (churn, risk, evolution) that are applicable to the wider Web, and this benchmark framework can guide next-generation temporal analytics research.

Abstract: Temporal Web analytics increasingly relies on large-scale, longitudinal data to understand how users, content, and systems evolve over time. A rapidly growing frontier is the \emph{Temporal Web3}: decentralized platforms whose behavior is recorded as immutable, time-stamped event streams. Despite the richness of this data, the field lacks shared, reproducible benchmarks that capture real-world temporal dynamics, specifically censoring and non-stationarity, across extended horizons. This absence slows methodological progress and limits the transfer of techniques between Web3 and broader Web domains. In this paper, we present the \textit{FinSurvival Challenge 2025} as a case study in benchmarking \emph{temporal Web3 intelligence}. Using 21.8 million transaction records from the Aave v3 protocol, the challenge operationalized 16 survival prediction tasks to model user behavior transitions.We detail the benchmark design and the winning solutions, highlighting how domain-aware temporal feature construction significantly outperformed generic modeling approaches. Furthermore, we distill lessons for next-generation temporal benchmarks, arguing that Web3 systems provide a high-fidelity sandbox for studying temporal challenges, such as churn, risk, and evolution that are fundamental to the wider Web.

</details>


### [304] [MetaOthello: A Controlled Study of Multiple World Models in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23164)
*Aviral Chawla,Galen Hall,Juniper Lovato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaOthello introduces a suite of Othello variants to study how transformers organize multiple world models in a shared representation space when trained on mixed data, finding that models converge on mostly shared board-state representations rather than isolated sub-models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models must handle multiple generative processes, but mechanistic interpretability studies capabilities in isolation. It remains unclear how a single transformer organizes multiple, potentially conflicting world models. Previous experiments on Othello playing neural networks focused on single games with single rule sets.

Method: Introduce MetaOthello, a controlled suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but different rules or tokenizations. Train small GPTs on mixed-variant data to study representation organization. Use linear probes to intervene on internal states, compare representations across isomorphic games with token remapping, and analyze layer behavior when rules partially overlap.

Result: Transformers trained on mixed-game data do not partition capacity into isolated sub-models. Instead, they converge on mostly shared board-state representations that transfer causally across variants. Linear probes trained on one variant can intervene on another's internal state with effectiveness approaching matched probes. For isomorphic games with token remapping, representations are equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation generalizing across layers. When rules partially overlap, early layers maintain game-agnostic representations, middle layers identify game identity, and later layers specialize.

Conclusion: MetaOthello offers a path toward understanding not just whether transformers learn world models, but how they organize many at once, demonstrating that transformers can maintain shared representations while handling multiple variant-specific processing.

Abstract: Foundation models must handle multiple generative processes, yet mechanistic interpretability largely studies capabilities in isolation; it remains unclear how a single transformer organizes multiple, potentially conflicting "world models". Previous experiments on Othello playing neural-networks test world-model learning but focus on a single game with a single set of rules. We introduce MetaOthello, a controlled suite of Othello variants with shared syntax but different rules or tokenizations, and train small GPTs on mixed-variant data to study how multiple world models are organized in a shared representation space. We find that transformers trained on mixed-game data do not partition their capacity into isolated sub-models; instead, they converge on a mostly shared board-state representation that transfers causally across variants. Linear probes trained on one variant can intervene on another's internal state with effectiveness approaching that of matched probes. For isomorphic games with token remapping, representations are equivalent up to a single orthogonal rotation that generalizes across layers. When rules partially overlap, early layers maintain game-agnostic representations while a middle layer identifies game identity, and later layers specialize. MetaOthello offers a path toward understanding not just whether transformers learn world models, but how they organize many at once.

</details>


### [305] [Induction Meets Biology: Mechanisms of Repeat Detection in Protein Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23179)
*Gal Kesten-Pomeranz,Yaniv Nikankin,Anja Reusch,Tomer Tsaban,Ora Schueler-Furman,Yonatan Belinkov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Protein sequences are abundant in repeating segments, both as exact copies and as approximate segments with mutations. These repeats are important for protein structure and function, motivating decades of algorithmic work on repeat identification. Recent work has shown that protein language models (PLMs) identify repeats, by examining their behavior in masked-token prediction. To elucidate their internal mechanisms, we investigate how PLMs detect both exact and approximate repeats. We find that the mechanism for approximate repeats functionally subsumes that of exact repeats. We then characterize this mechanism, revealing two main stages: PLMs first build feature representations using both general positional attention heads and biologically specialized components, such as neurons that encode amino-acid similarity. Then, induction heads attend to aligned tokens across repeated segments, promoting the correct answer. Our results reveal how PLMs solve this biological task by combining language-based pattern matching with specialized biological knowledge, thereby establishing a basis for studying more complex evolutionary processes in PLMs.

</details>


### [306] [Closing the gap on tabular data with Fourier and Implicit Categorical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23182)
*Marius Dragoi,Florin Gogianu,Elena Burceanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep learning underperforms tree-based methods on tabular data due to categorical feature interactions and smoothness bias. This paper proposes statistical feature preprocessing and Learned Fourier to mitigate these issues, enabling deep models to match or surpass XGBoost performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tree-based methods (e.g., XGBoost) outperform neural networks on tabular data. The authors hypothesize this gap stems from: (1) tree methods' ability to model non-linear interactions from categorical features, and (2) neural networks' bias toward uniform numerical processing and smooth solutions that don't align with tabular data properties.

Method: Two key contributions: (1) Statistical-based feature preprocessing to identify features strongly correlated with the target after discretization; (2) Learned Fourier to mitigate the deep learning bias toward overly-smooth solutions.

Result: The proposed feature preprocessing significantly boosts deep learning performance. The approach enables deep models to achieve performance that closely matches or surpasses XGBoost on a comprehensive tabular data benchmark.

Conclusion: By addressing the specific limitations of neural networks on tabular data—particularly their inability to leverage categorical feature interactions and over-smoothing bias—this work demonstrates that deep learning can successfully compete with tree-based methods, effectively conquering the "unconquered castle" of tabular data applications.

Abstract: While Deep Learning has demonstrated impressive results in applications on various data types, it continues to lag behind tree-based methods when applied to tabular data, often referred to as the last "unconquered castle" for neural networks. We hypothesize that a significant advantage of tree-based methods lies in their intrinsic capability to model and exploit non-linear interactions induced by features with categorical characteristics. In contrast, neural-based methods exhibit biases toward uniform numerical processing of features and smooth solutions, making it challenging for them to effectively leverage such patterns. We address this performance gap by using statistical-based feature processing techniques to identify features that are strongly correlated with the target once discretized. We further mitigate the bias of deep models for overly-smooth solutions, a bias that does not align with the inherent properties of the data, using Learned Fourier. We show that our proposed feature preprocessing significantly boosts the performance of deep learning models and enables them to achieve a performance that closely matches or surpasses XGBoost on a comprehensive tabular data benchmark.

</details>


### [307] [Efficient Real-Time Adaptation of ROMs for Unsteady Flows Using Data Assimilation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23188)
*Ismaël Zighed,Andrea Nóvoa,Luca Magri,Taraneh Sayadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An efficient retraining strategy for parameterized Reduced Order Models (ROMs) using a VAE-transformer architecture that enables real-time adaptation to out-of-sample parameter regions with minimal computational cost and sparse observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ROMs require full retraining for new parameter regimes, which is computationally expensive. Existing methods struggle to adapt to out-of-sample parameters while maintaining accuracy. There's a need for lightweight, real-time adaptation procedures that work with limited data.

Method: A parameterized ROM architecture with encode-process-decode structure: (1) Variational Autoencoder (VAE) for dimensionality reduction and probabilistic sampling; (2) Transformer network to evolve latent states, capturing temporal dependencies and parameter effects (Reynolds number). The approach uses ensemble Kalman filtering for data assimilation and employs attention mechanisms.

Result: The method attains accuracy comparable to full retraining while requiring only a fraction of computational time. The probabilistic formulation supports ensemble generation and uncertainty quantification. Analysis shows latent manifold distortion is the dominant error source, allowing retraining limited to the autoencoder only.

Conclusion: The proposed lightweight retraining strategy enables real-time adaptation with very sparse fine-tuning data. The architecture successfully balances accuracy and computational efficiency, with the main improvement opportunity being latent manifold distortion rather than latent dynamics changes.

Abstract: We propose an efficient retraining strategy for a parameterized Reduced Order Model (ROM) that attains accuracy comparable to full retraining while requiring only a fraction of the computational time and relying solely on sparse observations of the full system. The architecture employs an encode-process-decode structure: a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to perform dimensionality reduction, and a transformer network to evolve the latent states and model the dynamics. The ROM is parameterized by an external control variable, the Reynolds number in the Navier-Stokes setting, with the transformer exploiting attention mechanisms to capture both temporal dependencies and parameter effects. The probabilistic VAE enables stochastic sampling of trajectory ensembles, providing predictive means and uncertainty quantification through the first two moments. After initial training on a limited set of dynamical regimes, the model is adapted to out-of-sample parameter regions using only sparse data. Its probabilistic formulation naturally supports ensemble generation, which we employ within an ensemble Kalman filtering framework to assimilate data and reconstruct full-state trajectories from minimal observations. We further show that, for the dynamical system considered, the dominant source of error in out-of-sample forecasts stems from distortions of the latent manifold rather than changes in the latent dynamics. Consequently, retraining can be limited to the autoencoder, allowing for a lightweight, computationally efficient, real-time adaptation procedure with very sparse fine-tuning data.

</details>


### [308] [Tell Me What To Learn: Generalizing Neural Memory to be Controllable in Natural Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23201)
*Max S. Bennett,Thomas P. Zollo,Richard Zemel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a generalized neural memory system that enables flexible, instruction-based updates allowing adaptive agents to selectively learn from heterogeneous information sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ML models deployed in non-stationary environments require continual adaptation, but existing methods have limitations: continual fine-tuning and in-context learning are costly/brittle, while neural memory methods assume fixed objectives and homogeneous information streams, providing users no control over what the model remembers or ignores over time.
